figures.name: Ahmed Abu Risha
figures.title: Sheikh and Tribal Leader
figures.roleDescription: Leader of the Anbar Salvation Council and political coalition leader
figures.significance: Led the Anbar Awakening movement against al-Qaeda and became a prominent Sunni political leader in Iraq.
figures.biography:
Sheikh Ahmed Bezaa Abu Risha was born in 1969 as a member of the Dulaim tribe and is three years older than his brother Abdul Sattar. Following his brother Abdul Sattar's assassination by a roadside bomb in September 2007, Ahmed was selected by the sheiks of Anbar province to take over leadership of the Anbar Salvation Council. He met with President Barack Obama both before and after the 2008 Presidential Election and established a newspaper entitled Sahwat al-Iraq (Iraq's Awakening).

In politics, Abu Risha's Iraq Awakening and Independents National Alliance was the most successful coalition in the 2009 Anbar governorate election, winning 8 of 29 seats. However, the Alliance fared poorly in the 2010 parliamentary election, winning only 2.66% of the vote and 4 seats, with many votes going to Ayad Allawi's Iraqi National Movement. Abu Risha has been critical of Iranian interference in Iraq and supports secular and nationalistic parties over religious ones.
achievements: Led the Anbar Awakening movement after his brother's death
Most successful political coalition in 2009 Anbar governorate election
Met with President Barack Obama multiple times
Established Iraq's Awakening newspaper

figures.name: Abdul Sattar Abu Risha
figures.title: Sheikh and Tribal Leader
figures.roleDescription: Leader of the Sahawat al-Anbar (Anbar Awakening) alliance against al-Qaeda
figures.significance: Founded the Anbar Salvation Council that opposed al-Qaeda in Iraq and significantly reduced violence in Anbar province.
figures.biography:
Abdul Sattar Abu Risha was the grandson of a tribal leader in the 1920 Iraqi Revolt and son of an officer who served in the Anglo-Iraqi War in 1941. He was the leader of the Abu-Risha tribe and reportedly ran a construction and import-export business with offices in Amman, Jordan and Dubai. During the Iraq insurgency, as al-Qaeda fighters tightened their grip on Ramadi and became increasingly repressive, they killed Abdul Sattar's father and two brothers. In late summer 2006, he began enlisting fellow sheiks in Sahawat al-Anbar and encouraging tribe members to join the local police force.

With U.S. forces providing security for initial meetings at his compound in western Ramadi, these gatherings grew into the Anbar Salvation Council by fall 2006, eventually counting 41 clans from Anbar province by March 2007. The development led to a sharp reduction of violence in the province and forced many al-Qaeda fighters to flee to other regions of Iraq. Abdul Sattar was assassinated on September 13, 2007, along with three guards by an improvised explosive device near his house in Ramadi. The Islamic State of Iraq claimed responsibility for the attack.
achievements: Founded the Sahawat al-Anbar (Anbar Awakening)
Formed alliance of 41 clans against al-Qaeda
Significantly reduced violence in Anbar province
Led successful tribal opposition to al-Qaeda in Iraq

figures.name: Zulaikha Abu Risha
figures.title: Poet and Women's Rights Activist
figures.roleDescription: Prominent Jordanian poet, writer, and advocate for gender-inclusive Arabic language
figures.significance: A leading voice in Arabic feminist literature and advocate for making the Arabic language more gender-inclusive.
figures.biography:
Zulaikha Abd ar-Rahman Abu Risha was born in 1942 in Acre, describing herself as having Palestinian, Jordanian, and Syrian roots. She studied Arabic literature at the University of Jordan, graduating with a bachelor's degree in 1966 and a master's in 1989, later pursuing a doctorate at the University of Exeter where she wrote her thesis on 'Women in Arabic Feminist Literature'. She has been considered a prominent member of the Jordanian literary scene as both a poet and fiction writer.

In 1987, Abu Risha published the short story collection 'In the Cell,' winning a prize from the University of Jordan. She has written at least 10 books of poetry beginning in 1998, autobiographical essays, children's literature, and academic studies. Her work extends to feminist criticism, literature, art, and gender and language studies. She has served as editor of magazines, director of feminist publishing houses, and as a university lecturer and director of the Center for Women's Studies in Amman. Her advocacy for women's rights since the early 1980s has made her a target of extremist groups and lawsuits from Amman's Public Prosecution Office.
achievements: Published over 10 books of poetry and numerous literary works
Won University of Jordan prize for short story collection 'In the Cell'
Served as judge for International Prize for Arabic Fiction in 2019
Authored books on gender-inclusive Arabic language
Produced 'Timeless Tales: Folktales Told by Syrian Refugees'

figures.name: Ahmad bin Said al-Busaidi
figures.title: Imam of Oman
figures.roleDescription: Founder of the Al Bu Said dynasty
figures.significance: Founded the Al Bu Said dynasty in 1744 and liberated Oman from Persian occupation.
figures.biography:
Ahmad bin Said al-Busaidi was born in Adam, Oman in 1694, the son of Sa'id bin Ahmad Al-Busaidi. He came from the Al Bu Sa'id, a small Hinawi tribe from the interior of Oman and was great-great-grandson of Mubarak al-Saidi al-Azdy of the Banu Hiba. Beginning as a wood vendor, he worked his way up to become a leading merchant of the port city of Sohar and was appointed governor of this city in 1737.

When Persian forces under Nader Shah occupied much of Oman between 1742-1744, Ahmad defended Sohar during a nine-month siege and eventually negotiated terms with the Persian commander. By 1747, he had invited the remaining Persian troops to a banquet at his fort in Barka where he massacred them, effectively ending Persian occupation. After defeating rival claimant Bal'arab bin Himyar in 1749, the Ibadi tribes of Oman elected Ahmad bin Said as their Imam on 9 June 1749 at Rustaq.

Ahmad ruled for 39 generally peaceful years from his seat at Rustaq, encouraging the development of agriculture and maritime trading while maintaining Oman's first permanent army and navy. He reestablished Oman's leading position among the Persian Gulf states and attempted to gain control of the strategic Strait of Hormuz in the late 1770s. He died on 15 December 1783 at Al-Batinah Fort, Rustaq, where he is buried near the Great Mosque.
achievements: Founded the Al Bu Said dynasty in 1744
Liberated Oman from Persian occupation
Established Oman's first permanent army and navy
Reestablished Oman as a leading Persian Gulf power

figures.name: Said bin Sultan
figures.title: Sultan of Muscat and Oman
figures.roleDescription: Known as 'Said the Great', expanded Omani empire to its zenith
figures.significance: Extended Oman's borders to include much of Southeast Africa and established Zanzibar as a second capital.
figures.biography:
Said bin Sultan, known as 'Said the Great', was the son of Sultan bin Ahmed and came to power at age sixteen after conspiring to have regent Badr bin Saif assassinated in a dramatic duel at Bait al Nu'man castle. He assumed government control and held it for fifty-two years until his death, taking Oman to its zenith as a commercial and political maritime power.

During his reign, Said extended Oman's borders to include Mombasa and parts of the Southeast African coast, Zanzibar, Pemba Island, Mafia Island, Lamu Archipelago, Cape Delgado, Bandar-Abbas and the southern Iranian coast, and Gwadar province. In 1832, he made Zanzibar his second capital and established what is now Stone Town. He initiated large-scale cultivation of cloves and revitalized production of rice and sugar cane, establishing what could be considered the first international market on Zanzibar.

Said signed a historic treaty of friendship and trade with the United States on 21 September 1833, and in 1840 sent the ship Al-Sultanah to New York - the first Arab envoy to visit the New World. The explorer Richard Francis Burton called him 'as shrewd, liberal and enlightened a prince as Arabia has ever produced.' Said died on 19 October 1856 aboard his ship Kitorie at age sixty-five, suffering from an old battle wound and dysentery.
achievements: Expanded Omani empire to include Southeast Africa and Zanzibar
Established Zanzibar as second capital in 1832
Signed first US-Arab trade treaty after Morocco in 1833
Sent first Arab diplomatic mission to America in 1840

figures.name: Qaboos bin Said
figures.title: Sultan of Oman
figures.roleDescription: Longest-serving leader in Middle East, modernized Oman
figures.significance: Ruled for almost 50 years and transformed Oman from an isolated, underdeveloped country into a modern state.
figures.biography:
Qaboos bin Said was born on 18 November 1940 in Salalah as the only son of Sultan Said bin Taimur and Mazoon bint Ahmad Al Mashani. He received his education in India and England, attending Bury St Edmunds before entering the Royal Military Academy Sandhurst. After graduating in 1962, he served with the British Army's 1st Battalion The Cameronians in Germany. Upon returning to Oman in 1966, he was placed under virtual house arrest by his father at Al Hosn Palace in Salalah.

On 23 July 1970, Qaboos overthrew his father in a British-supported coup, ascending to the throne with the aim of ending Oman's isolation and modernizing the country using oil revenues. He changed the country's name from 'Muscat and Oman' to 'the Sultanate of Oman' and immediately faced the Dhofar Rebellion, which he successfully defeated with international help by 1976. Under his rule, Oman was transformed from having only 10 km of paved roads and three schools to a modern state with extensive infrastructure, healthcare, and education systems.

Qaboos established an absolute monarchy while implementing careful modernization and maintaining Oman's neutrality in regional conflicts. He often served as a mediator between the US and Iran, facilitated the release of American hostages, and maintained diplomatic relations across the political spectrum. He never married after his 1979 divorce and had no children. Suffering from colon cancer, he died on 10 January 2020 and was succeeded by his cousin Haitham bin Tariq according to his sealed letter of succession.
achievements: Modernized Oman's infrastructure, education and healthcare systems
Abolished slavery in his first year of rule
Successfully ended the Dhofar Rebellion
Served as mediator in US-Iran relations
Established Oman's policy of neutrality in regional conflicts

figures.name: Haitham bin Tariq Al Said
figures.title: Sultan of Oman
figures.roleDescription: Current Sultan of Oman since 2020
figures.significance: Became Sultan of Oman in 2020 following Qaboos bin Said's death, chosen as successor according to Qaboos's sealed letter.
figures.biography:
Haitham bin Tariq Al Said is the cousin of the late Sultan Qaboos and became the current Sultan of Oman on 11 January 2020. He was previously Minister of Heritage and National Culture and was considered one of the top three contenders to succeed Qaboos, along with his brothers Asa'ad bin Tariq and Shihab bin Tariq. He is the son of Sayyid Tariq bin Taimur, who was Oman's first prime minister and Qaboos's former father-in-law.

When Qaboos died without naming a public heir, the Royal Family Council opened the sealed letter containing Qaboos's choice for successor, which named Haitham bin Tariq as the intended heir. The royal court quickly announced their decision to defer to Qaboos's written choice. In 2021, Sultan Haitham created the position of Crown Prince of Oman by Royal Decree, appointing his eldest son Theyazin bin Haitham to the role, ensuring succession planning for the dynasty.
achievements: Became Sultan of Oman following constitutional succession process
Created the position of Crown Prince in 2021
Appointed his son as first Crown Prince of Oman

figures.name: Said bin Taimur
figures.title: Sultan of Muscat and Oman
figures.roleDescription: 13th Sultan, father of Qaboos, overthrown in 1970 coup
figures.significance: His conservative policies led to his overthrow by his son Qaboos in 1970, ending his isolationist rule.
figures.biography:
Said bin Taimur was born on 13 August 1910 and became the 13th Sultan of Muscat and Oman on 10 February 1932 at age 21, succeeding his father Taimur bin Feisal who had abdicated for financial reasons. He inherited an administration heavily in debt to Britain. He attended Mayo College in India from 1922-1927 and later studied in Baghdad. He secured British recognition of Oman's independence in 1951 and sold Gwadar to Pakistan for $1 million in 1958.

Despite oil wealth that could have modernized the country, Said became increasingly reclusive and implemented restrictive policies. By 1965, it was forbidden to smoke in public, play football, wear sunglasses, or speak to anyone for more than 15 minutes. The Dhofar province revolted in 1965 with Chinese and Arab nationalist support, followed by an assassination attempt in 1966 that made him even more paranoid. He kept his own son Qaboos under virtual house arrest and refused to speak to him for the last 14 months before the coup.

Under his rule, Oman had an under-5 mortality rate of 25%, widespread disease and malnutrition, only three schools, 5% literacy rate, and just 10 kilometers of paved roads. On 23 July 1970, Qaboos executed a successful British-supported coup against his father at the palace in Salalah. Said was exiled to London where he lived at the Dorchester Hotel until his death on 19 October 1972. He was initially buried at Brookwood Cemetery before his remains were returned to Oman.
achievements: Secured British recognition of Oman's independence in 1951
Sold Gwadar province to Pakistan in 1958
Maintained friendly relations with the United States during WWII

figures.name: Thuwaini bin Said
figures.title: Sultan of Muscat and Oman
figures.roleDescription: Sultan from 1856-1866, ruled Omani portion after empire's division
figures.significance: Became Sultan of Muscat and Oman after his father's death, presiding over the division of the Omani empire between Oman and Zanzibar.
figures.biography:
Thuwaini bin Said al-Busaidi was born in 1821 as the third son of Said bin Sultan. Unlike his brothers, Thuwaini was born in Oman and never visited Zanzibar, serving as his father's representative in Oman when Said was away in Zanzibar. He was appointed heir apparent on 23 July 1844 and had long acted as his father's Governor in Muscat and commander-in-chief of the Saidi forces.

After Said bin Sultan died in 1856, Thuwaini became Sultan of Muscat and Oman while his brother Majid took power in Zanzibar. This led to a struggle between the brothers, with Thuwaini refusing to acknowledge Majid and attempting to regain Zanzibar by force. The British government of India acted as arbitrator, and Lord Canning ruled that the empire should be divided into two separate sultanates: Zanzibar to Majid and Muscat and Oman to Thuwaini, formalized in the 1862 Zanzibar Guarantee Treaty signed in Paris.

Thuwaini insisted that Majid pay 40,000 Maria Theresa thalers annually as compensation for the economic loss caused by the severance of the Zanzibar connection, but the payment fell into arrears and ceased after a year. This left Muscat and Oman in a difficult financial situation, forcing Thuwaini to levy duties on various articles, creating discontent. He was married to his cousin Ralie and had several children. In 1866, he was rumored to have been killed by his own son, Sayyid Salim bin Thuwaini.
achievements: Presided over the formal division of Omani empire in 1862
Served as heir apparent and commander-in-chief under his father
Negotiated compensation payments from Zanzibar for Oman

figures.name: Tathi bint Mishan Al Faisal Al Jarba
figures.title: Wife of King Abdullah
figures.roleDescription: Wife of Saudi King Abdullah bin Abdulaziz
figures.significance: Mother of six children of King Abdullah of Saudi Arabia, including Prince Mishaal and Prince Turki.
figures.biography:
Tathi bint Mishan Al Faisal Al Jarba was one of the wives of King Abdullah bin Abdulaziz Al Saud of Saudi Arabia. She belonged to the prominent Al Jarba family of the Iraqi branch of the Shammar tribe. Her marriage to Abdullah was part of traditional tribal alliance-building between the Saudi royal family and major tribal confederations.

Tathi gave birth to six children with King Abdullah, including notable sons Prince Mishaal, who served as governor of the Makkah Province between 2013 and 2015, and Prince Turki, who was a pilot in the Royal Saudi Air Force and served as governor of the Riyadh Province from 2014 to 2015. She also had a daughter Princess Oraib. Through her children, Tathi's lineage became integrated into the Saudi royal family, strengthening ties between the Al Saud and the Shammar confederation.
achievements: Mother of Saudi princes including governors of major provinces

figures.name: Malka bint Saud bin Zaid Al Jarba Al Choumri
figures.title: Wife of King Abdullah
figures.roleDescription: Wife of Saudi King Abdullah bin Abdulaziz
figures.significance: Another wife of King Abdullah from the Al Jarba family, mother of three royal children including Prince Saad and Prince Sultan.
figures.biography:
Malka bint Saud bin Zaid Al Jarba Al Choumri was another wife of King Abdullah bin Abdulaziz Al Saud from the prominent Al Jarba family of the Shammar tribe. Her marriage to Abdullah represented the continued alliance between the Saudi royal family and the influential Shammar confederation.

Malka had three children with King Abdullah: Prince Saad, Princess Sahab, and Prince Sultan. Her marriage demonstrates the practice of multiple marriages within the same tribal family to strengthen political and tribal alliances. The Al Jarba family's connection to the Saudi royal house through multiple marriages reflects their significant status within the broader tribal hierarchy of the Arabian Peninsula.
achievements: Mother of three Saudi royal children

figures.name: Muslit bin Mutlag Al-Jarba
figures.title: Sheikh
figures.roleDescription: Shammar tribal leader killed in Wahhabi conflicts
figures.significance: His death in 1791 triggered a major migration of Shammar clans from Najd to Iraq under Al-Jarba leadership.
figures.biography:
Sheikh Muslit bin Mutlag Al-Jarba was a prominent leader of the Shammar tribe during the late 18th century when the tribe faced increasing pressure from Wahhabi expansion in the Arabian Peninsula. He led the tribe during a period of intense conflict with the emerging Saudi state and their Wahhabi allies.

Muslit's death in 1791 during the Wahhabi wars marked a turning point for the Shammar confederation. His killing triggered a major exodus of Shammar clans from their traditional territories in Najd to safer regions in southern Iraq. Under the leadership of the Al-Jarba family, particularly Mutlag Al-Jarba, large numbers of Shammar migrated northward to escape continued Wahhabi pressure and raids. This migration established the foundation for the significant Shammar presence in Iraq that continues to this day, where they became one of the country's largest tribal confederations.
achievements: Led Shammar resistance against early Wahhabi expansion

figures.name: Mutlag Al-Jarba
figures.title: Sheikh
figures.roleDescription: Leader of Shammar migration to Iraq
figures.significance: Led the major Shammar exodus from Arabia to Iraq in the 1790s following Wahhabi conflicts.
figures.biography:
Mutlag Al-Jarba was a crucial figure in Shammar tribal history who led the great migration of the tribe from their ancestral lands in Najd to Iraq in the 1790s. Following the death of Muslit bin Mutlag Al-Jarba in 1791, Mutlag took leadership of the Shammar and guided them to safer territories in southern Iraq to escape the advancing Wahhabi forces of the first Saudi state.

Mutlag successfully established Shammar settlements in southern Iraq and organized the tribe's resistance against continued Saudi raids. However, his leadership was cut short when he was killed in 1795 during the Battle of Alabyadh, some sources suggest, as Saudi forces continued their attacks even after the Shammar had relocated. His death marked the end of the initial phase of Shammar settlement in Iraq, but the foundation he laid enabled the tribe to become one of Iraq's most influential tribal confederations under subsequent Al-Jarba leadership.
achievements: Led the great Shammar migration to Iraq
Established Shammar presence in Mesopotamia

figures.name: Faris bin Mohammad Al-Jarba
figures.title: Sheikh
figures.roleDescription: Shammar leader in early 19th century Iraq
figures.significance: Ruled over 12,000 Shammar families in Iraq and successfully defeated the Al-Ubaid tribe in 1805.
figures.biography:
Faris bin Mohammad Al-Jarba emerged as a prominent leader of the Iraqi Shammar in the early 19th century, following in the footsteps of earlier Al-Jarba chiefs who had led the tribe's migration from Arabia. He took leadership after Faris Al-Jarba and commanded significant influence over the large Shammar population that had settled in the Mesopotamian region.

In 1805, Faris achieved a decisive military victory by defeating the Al-Ubaid tribe, which significantly strengthened Shammar control over their territories in the Jazirah plain between the Tigris and Euphrates rivers. By 1836, he ruled over approximately 12,000 Shammar families, making him one of the most powerful tribal leaders in Iraq. His leadership helped consolidate Shammar power in their new homeland and established them as a dominant force in Iraqi tribal politics.
achievements: Defeated Al-Ubaid tribe in 1805
Ruled over 12,000 Shammar families

figures.name: Ahmad Jarba
figures.title: Political Opposition Leader
figures.roleDescription: Syrian National Council member and founder of Syria's Tomorrow Movement
figures.significance: Founded Syria's Tomorrow Movement in 2016 and served as a key Syrian opposition leader during the civil war.
figures.biography:
Ahmad Jarba is a prominent Syrian opposition political leader and member of the Syrian National Council who founded the Syria's Tomorrow Movement in March 2016 in Cairo. The party was backed by Egypt and the United Arab Emirates and cooperates with the National Coalition for Syrian Revolutionary and Opposition Forces, although it maintains independence from the coalition. Jarba also works with the Syrian Democratic Council of the Autonomous Administration of North and East Syria.

The Syria's Tomorrow Movement under Jarba's leadership aims to decentralize Syria and implement pluralist democracy while opposing any partition of the state. The party considers all ethnic groups, including Alawites, to be essential components of the Syrian people and calls for greater inclusion in the opposition. In September 2016, Jarba met with Kurdish leaders to form agreements for governing northeastern Syria, and his movement signed cooperation agreements with various Syrian opposition groups including the National Coordination Committee for Democratic Change.
achievements: Founded Syria's Tomorrow Movement
Syrian National Council member
Negotiated Syrian opposition agreements

figures.name: Wasmiyah bint Abdul Rahman Al Mu'ammar
figures.nameAr: وسمية بنت عبد الرحمن آل معمر
figures.title: Mother of Prince Faisal bin Bandar
figures.roleDescription: Mother of a senior Saudi royal and governor
figures.significance: Mother of Prince Faisal bin Bandar bin Abdulaziz Al Saud, who served as governor of Al-Qassim Province and later Riyadh Province.
figures.biography:
Wasmiyah bint Abdul Rahman Al Mu'ammar was a member of the prominent Al Mu'ammar merchant family. She married Prince Bandar bin Abdulaziz, a son of Saudi Arabia's founder King Abdulaziz. Their eldest son, Prince Faisal, was born in 1945 and went on to have a distinguished career in Saudi government service.

As the mother of Prince Faisal bin Bandar, she was connected to the highest levels of Saudi governance through her son's roles as deputy governor and later governor of Asir Province, governor of Al-Qassim Province for over two decades, and eventually governor of Riyadh Province from 2015.

figures.name: Uthman ibn Mu'ammar
figures.title: Emir of Uyayna
figures.roleDescription: 18th century ruler of Uyayna who supported Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab's reforms
figures.significance: Emir of Uyayna who implemented the religious reforms of Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab, including the destruction of venerated tombs, before being forced to expel the reformer in 1744.
figures.biography:
Uthman ibn Mu'ammar was the emir (ruler) of Uyayna in central Arabia during the 18th century, when the town had become the leading settlement of the Nejd region. When the Islamic reformer Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab returned to Uyayna in 1740 and began preaching against what he considered religious innovations, Ibn Mu'ammar was convinced to implement these ideas.

Under Ibn Mu'ammar's authority, the tomb of Zayd ibn al-Khattab, brother of the second Muslim Caliph Omar, was leveled as part of the Wahhabi campaign against the veneration of Muslim saints. However, in 1744, the Banu Khalid tribe, who held considerable influence over Uyayna from their base in eastern Arabia, compelled Ibn Mu'ammar to expel Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab from the town. The reformer subsequently found refuge with the Al Saud clan in nearby Dir'iyyah, leading to the eventual Saudi conquest of the region.
achievements: Implemented Wahhabi religious reforms in Uyayna
Ordered the destruction of venerated tombs

figures.name: Ali bin Ibrahim Al-Naimi
figures.title: Former Saudi Minister of Petroleum and Mineral Resources
figures.roleDescription: Saudi Arabian politician and oil executive
figures.significance: Served as Saudi Arabian Minister of Petroleum and Mineral Resources from 1995 to 2016 and was the first Saudi to be president of Saudi Aramco.
figures.biography:
Ali bin Ibrahim Al-Naimi was born on August 2, 1935, in Ar-Rakah in Saudi Arabia's Eastern Province. Born to Ibrahim, a pearl diver of the Al-Naimi tribe, and Fatima, a Bedouin of the Ajman, his parents became divorced during the pregnancy. He lived the first eight years with his mother's and stepfather's tribe before moving to live with his father in Al-Hasa. He was hired by Aramco as a junior clerk on December 6, 1947, while still attending the company's Jebel School.

Under Aramco's training programs, he studied at International College, Beirut, and the American University of Beirut, before earning a Bachelor of Science degree in geology at Lehigh University in 1962 and a Master of Science degree in hydrology and economic geology at Stanford University. He joined Aramco's exploration and production department as a geologist in 1964 and rose through the ranks, becoming president of Saudi Aramco in November 1983, being the first Saudi to hold that position. In 1988, he assumed responsibilities as CEO.

He became the minister of Petroleum and Mineral Resources on August 2, 1995 (his 60th birthday), replacing Hisham Nazer, becoming the first oil executive to hold the office. During his tenure, he became the de facto leader of OPEC and advocated for controversial strategies including allowing the oil market to rebalance itself at lower price levels. He completed his service as oil minister in May 2016 and was replaced by Khaled Al-Falih. King Salman named Al-Naimi adviser to the Royal Court, and he published his memoirs 'Out of the Desert' in 2016.
achievements: First Saudi to serve as president of Saudi Aramco
First oil executive to serve as Saudi Minister of Petroleum and Mineral Resources
Named one of the most influential people in the world by Time magazine in 2008
Included in the inaugural 50 Most Influential ranking by Bloomberg Markets magazine in 2011

figures.name: Abdulla Majid Al Naimi
figures.title: Former Guantanamo Bay Detainee
figures.roleDescription: Bahraini citizen formerly held in extrajudicial detention
figures.significance: Was one of three Bahraini detainees released from Guantanamo Bay in November 2005 and later commented on the 2006 suicides at the facility.
figures.biography:
Abdulla Majid Al Naimi was born on March 9, 1982, in Manama, Bahrain. He has stated that he was abducted by Pashtun tribesmen in Afghanistan in late 2001 and then handed over to Pakistani authorities in return for a cash reward. After being moved around for a while, he eventually arrived at a prison at Kohat before being transferred to American custody and flown to Guantanamo Bay.

During his detention, a Combatant Status Review Tribunal was convened to determine his status as an 'enemy combatant.' The tribunal accused him of being a Taliban fighter who had traveled from Bahrain to Afghanistan on September 13, 2001, to fight for the Taliban. Witness statements included testimony from Mohammed Salman Al-Khalifa, a cousin of a member of the Bahrain royal family, stating that Al Naimi was asked to travel to Pakistan and Afghanistan to look for Salman Al Khalifa when he went missing in August 2001.

Represented by attorney Joshua Colangelo-Bryan, Al-Naimi was released and sent back to Bahrain in November 2005 along with two other Bahraini detainees. He later commented on the deaths of three detainees at Guantanamo in June 2006, stating that two of them were told by interrogators that they were not regarded as a threat and could expect to be released. In late October 2008, he was arrested again in Saudi Arabia but was eventually released without charge and returned to Bahrain on April 21, 2016.

figures.name: Ali bin Majid bin Ali Hassan Al Majid Al-Naimi
figures.nameAr: علي ماجد علي حسن الماجد النعيمي
figures.title: Bahraini Lawyer and Politician
figures.roleDescription: Member of Bahrain's Council of Representatives
figures.significance: Became the first person to win election as a deputy in the House of Representatives while his father held a ministerial portfolio.
figures.biography:
Ali bin Majid bin Ali Hassan Al Majid Al-Naimi is a Bahraini lawyer and politician who was sworn into the Council of Representatives on December 12, 2018, representing the Seventh District of the Southern Governorate. He earned a master's degree from the Applied Science University in 2012 and followed it up with a doctorate from the Institute of Arab Research and Studies (IARS), a division of the Arab League's Arab League Educational, Cultural and Scientific Organization. His dissertation was a comparative study of the state's responsibility for public servants' mistakes.

In the 2018 Bahraini general election, Al-Naimi ran to represent the Seventh District in the Southern Governorate in the Council of Representatives. He received 1,956 votes for 28.07% in the first round on November 24, necessitating a runoff on December 1, in which he defeated his opponent, Ahmed Al-Tamimi, with 3,267 votes for 52.81%. His election was historically significant as he became the first person to win election as a deputy in the House of Representatives while his father, Majid bin Ali Al-Naimi, held a ministerial portfolio as Minister of Education.

His career has been marked by controversy, including a 2016 report from the Financial and Administrative Control Department showing his absence for seven unaccounted months from work at the National Institute for Human Rights during which he earned BD17,542 in salaries. During the 2018 Bahrain forged degree scandal, his doctorate's provenance from the IARS came under scrutiny, as the Ministry of Education had issued a statement in 2010 stating that degrees from the institute would not be recognized.
achievements: First person to win election as deputy while father held ministerial position

figures.name: Majid bin Ali Al-Naimi
figures.title: Bahraini Minister of Education
figures.roleDescription: Bahraini academic and government minister
figures.significance: Has served as Bahrain's Minister of Education since 2002.
figures.biography:
Majid bin Ali Al-Naimi is a Bahraini academic who has served as Minister of Education since 2002. He is the father of Ali bin Majid Al-Naimi, who made history in 2018 by becoming the first person to win election as a deputy in Bahrain's House of Representatives while his father held a ministerial portfolio.

During his long tenure as Minister of Education, Al-Naimi has overseen significant developments in Bahrain's educational sector. His ministry was involved in the 2018 controversy regarding degrees from the Institute of Arab Research and Studies (IARS), where the ministry had issued a statement in 2010 directing applicants to avoid registering at the institute due to significant reservations about its accreditation.
achievements: Long-serving Minister of Education since 2002

figures.name: Saadi Al Munla
figures.title: Former Prime Minister of Lebanon
figures.roleDescription: Lebanese politician and former Prime Minister
figures.significance: Served as Prime Minister of Lebanon in 1946 and held various ministerial positions.
figures.biography:
Saadi Al Munla was born on November 4, 1890, in Tripoli, Lebanon, into a Sunni family. He received a law degree and worked as a lawyer by profession. He was a protégé of Rashid Karami and became a member of the Independence Party headed by Abdul Hamid Karami. He then became a member of the Lebanese parliament.

In 1945, he was appointed minister to the cabinet led by Prime Minister Sami Solh. Munla and two other cabinet members who were also members of the Independence Party, Ahmed Asad and Jamil Talhouk, resigned from office in May 1946. On May 22, 1946, Munla was appointed Prime Minister under President Bishara Khoury, replacing Sami Solh in the post. During his premiership, Munla also assumed the position of economy minister.

Munla remained in office until December 14, 1946, when Riad Solh succeeded him as prime minister. He died in December 1975, having served his country in various political capacities throughout his career.
achievements: Served as Prime Minister of Lebanon (1946)
Served as Minister of Economy
Member of Lebanese Parliament
Member of the Independence Party

figures.name: Hamad bin Abdullah Al Sharqi
figures.title: Sheikh and Ruler of Fujairah
figures.roleDescription: Founder ruler who declared independence from Sharjah
figures.significance: First independent ruler of Fujairah who declared independence from Sharjah in 1901.
figures.biography:
Sheikh Hamad bin Abdullah Al Sharqi was the chief of the Sharqiyin tribe and the founding ruler of the independent Emirate of Fujairah. In 1901, he declared independence from Sharjah, which was recognized by a number of the Trucial Sheikhs and also by Muscat, but not by the British, who were frequently provoked by the independently minded ruler.

At the time of his rule, the Emirate of Fujairah consisted of some 150 houses and 3,000 date palms, and its people lived mainly through pearling and date cultivation. He ruled from 1879 to 1936, establishing the Al Sharqi dynasty's control over Fujairah and laying the foundation for the emirate's eventual recognition by Britain in 1952.
achievements: Declared independence of Fujairah from Sharjah in 1901
Founded the independent Emirate of Fujairah

figures.name: Saif bin Hamad Al Sharqi
figures.title: Sheikh and Ruler of Fujairah
figures.roleDescription: Second ruler of Fujairah
figures.significance: Second ruler of Fujairah who briefly ruled between his father and son.
figures.biography:
Sheikh Saif bin Hamad Al Sharqi was the son of Sheikh Hamad bin Abdullah Al Sharqi, the founder of independent Fujairah. He succeeded his father as ruler of Fujairah in 1936 and ruled for a brief period until 1938.

His reign was a transitional period in Fujairah's history, maintaining the emirate's independence during a time when the British still had not officially recognized Fujairah's sovereignty. He was succeeded by his son Sheikh Mohammed bin Hamad Al Sharqi in 1938.

figures.name: Mohammed bin Hamad Al Sharqi
figures.title: Sheikh and Ruler of Fujairah
figures.roleDescription: Third ruler of Fujairah who achieved British recognition
figures.significance: Ruler who achieved British recognition for Fujairah in 1952 and led the emirate into the UAE federation.
figures.biography:
Sheikh Mohammed bin Hamad Al Sharqi ruled Fujairah from 1938 to 1974 and was instrumental in achieving international recognition for the emirate. In 1952, the Emirate of Fujairah entered into treaty relations with Britain under his leadership, becoming the last of the emirates to join the Trucial States. The British government had withheld this recognition for over fifty years and only granted it because the oil exploration company Petroleum Concessions Limited (PCL) needed to sign a concession with a recognized ruler.

Under his rule, Fujairah joined the United Arab Emirates on December 2, 1971. He was the father of the current ruler, Sheikh Hamad bin Mohammed Al Sharqi. His reign spanned the crucial period of transition from British protectorate status to full membership in the newly formed UAE federation.
achievements: Achieved British recognition for Fujairah in 1952
Led Fujairah into the UAE federation in 1971

figures.name: Hamad bin Mohammed Al Sharqi
figures.title: Sheikh and Ruler of Fujairah
figures.roleDescription: Current ruler of Fujairah and former UAE Minister
figures.significance: Current ruler of Fujairah since 1974 and first UAE Minister of Agriculture and Fisheries.
figures.biography:
Sheikh Hamad bin Mohammed Al Sharqi was born on February 22, 1949, as the son of Sheikh Mohammed bin Hamad Al Sharqi, former ruler of Fujairah. His mother, Sheikha Fatima bint Rashid Al Nuaimi, was a princess of Ajman and sister of Sheikh Humaid bin Rashid Al Nuaimi. He studied Arabic at Eastbourne School of English in East Sussex, United Kingdom, from 1969 to 1970 and attended the Mons Officer Cadet School in 1970.

From 1971 to 1974, he served as the first Minister for Agriculture and Fisheries of the UAE. In 1974, Sheikh Hamad succeeded his father to the throne of Fujairah after his father's death. He is fluent in both English and Arabic due to his British education and frequently represents the UAE in international seminars and conferences. He is married to Sheikha Fatima bint Thani Al Maktoum, a member of the Al-Maktoum family that rules Dubai.
achievements: First UAE Minister of Agriculture and Fisheries (1971-1974)
Current ruler of Fujairah since 1974
Frequent UAE representative at international conferences

figures.name: Mohammed bin Hamad bin Mohammed Al Sharqi
figures.nameAr: محمد بن حمد بن محمد الشرقي
figures.title: Crown Prince of Fujairah
figures.roleDescription: Heir apparent to the throne of Fujairah
figures.significance: Crown Prince of Fujairah and eldest son of the current ruler.
figures.biography:
Sheikh Mohammed bin Hamad bin Mohammed Al Sharqi was born on April 1, 1986, and is the Crown Prince of the Emirate of Fujairah. He is the eldest son of Sheikh Hamad bin Mohammed Al Sharqi, the current ruler of Fujairah. He attended Milton Abbey School, a boarding independent school in Dorset, England, and is a graduate of Webster University, London.

In February 2009, he married his second cousin Sheikha Latifa bint Mohammed bin Rashid Al Maktoum, daughter of the current ruler of Dubai and sister to both the crown prince of Dubai and its deputy ruler. They have five children together. The Crown Prince is descended from three of the six royal families that rule the UAE, strengthening inter-emirate family ties.
achievements: Crown Prince of Fujairah
Graduate of Webster University London

figures.name: Sara bint Hamad bin Mohammed Al Sharqi
figures.title: Sheikha
figures.roleDescription: Daughter of the ruler of Fujairah
figures.significance: Eldest daughter of the current ruler of Fujairah and American University of Sharjah graduate.
figures.biography:
Sheikha Sara bint Hamad bin Mohammed Al Sharqi was born in 1985 as the eldest daughter of Sheikh Hamad bin Mohammed Al Sharqi, the current ruler of Fujairah, and Sheikha Fatima bint Thani Al Maktoum. She is a graduate of the American University of Sharjah, demonstrating the family's commitment to higher education.

As a member of the ruling family of Fujairah, she represents the younger generation of Emirati royalty who have received international education while maintaining their cultural heritage and family traditions.
achievements: Graduate of American University of Sharjah

figures.name: Rashid bin Hamad bin Mohammed Al Sharqi
figures.title: Sheikh
figures.roleDescription: Head of media department of Fujairah
figures.significance: Son of the ruler who heads Fujairah's media department and holds a PhD in economics.
figures.biography:
Sheikh Rashid bin Hamad bin Mohammed Al Sharqi was born in 1987 as the second son of Sheikh Hamad bin Mohammed Al Sharqi, the current ruler of Fujairah. He holds a PhD in economics from London, reflecting the family's emphasis on advanced education and expertise.

He currently serves as the head of the media department of Fujairah, playing an important role in the emirate's communications and public relations efforts. His academic background in economics and his media responsibilities position him as a key figure in Fujairah's modern development and international outreach.
achievements: PhD in economics from London
Head of media department of Fujairah

figures.name: Shamsa bint Hamad bin Mohammed Al Sharqi
figures.title: Sheikha
figures.roleDescription: Head of various charities in Fujairah
figures.significance: Daughter of the ruler who leads charitable activities in Fujairah.
figures.biography:
Sheikha Shamsa bint Hamad bin Mohammed Al Sharqi was born in 1988 as a daughter of Sheikh Hamad bin Mohammed Al Sharqi, the current ruler of Fujairah. She is a graduate of Zayed University in Dubai, one of the UAE's leading higher education institutions.

She currently heads various charities in Fujairah, demonstrating the royal family's commitment to social responsibility and community welfare. Her charitable work contributes to the social development of the emirate and reflects the traditional values of Arabian leadership in caring for the community.
achievements: Graduate of Zayed University Dubai
Head of various charities in Fujairah

figures.name: Madiyah bint Hamad bin Mohammed Al Sharqi
figures.title: Sheikha
figures.roleDescription: Fashion designer
figures.significance: Daughter of the ruler who works as a fashion designer.
figures.biography:
Sheikha Madiyah bint Hamad bin Mohammed Al Sharqi was born in 1989 as a daughter of Sheikh Hamad bin Mohammed Al Sharqi, the current ruler of Fujairah, and Sheikha Fatima bint Thani Al Maktoum. She has pursued a career as a fashion designer, representing the creative and entrepreneurial spirit of the younger generation of Emirati royalty.

Her work in fashion design showcases the modern aspirations of the Al Sharqi family while maintaining their cultural identity. She represents the diversification of interests and careers among contemporary Emirati royal family members.
achievements: Fashion designer

figures.name: Maktoum bin Hamad bin Mohammed Al Sharqi
figures.title: Sheikh
figures.roleDescription: Military officer and Sandhurst graduate
figures.significance: Youngest son of the ruler and graduate of the prestigious Royal Military Academy Sandhurst.
figures.biography:
Sheikh Maktoum bin Hamad bin Mohammed Al Sharqi was born in 1991 as the youngest son of Sheikh Hamad bin Mohammed Al Sharqi, the current ruler of Fujairah. He is a graduate of the Royal Military Academy Sandhurst, the prestigious British military academy that has trained many members of royal families and military leaders worldwide.

His military education at Sandhurst reflects the family's tradition of service and leadership, as well as their strong ties with British institutions. As the youngest male member of the immediate ruling family, he represents the continuation of the Al Sharqi dynasty's commitment to professional excellence and international education.
achievements: Graduate of Royal Military Academy Sandhurst

figures.name: Hamad bin Mohammed bin Hamad bin Mohammed Al Sharqi
figures.title: Sheikh
figures.roleDescription: Grandson of the ruler of Fujairah
figures.significance: Eldest son of the Crown Prince, representing the next generation of the Al Sharqi dynasty.
figures.biography:
Sheikh Hamad bin Mohammed bin Hamad bin Mohammed Al Sharqi was born on December 29, 2009, as the eldest son of Crown Prince Mohammed bin Hamad bin Mohammed Al Sharqi and Sheikha Latifa bint Mohammed bin Rashid Al Maktoum. He is the grandson of the current ruler of Fujairah and represents the future generation of the Al Sharqi dynasty.

As the eldest son of the Crown Prince, he is positioned in the line of succession for the emirate. His lineage connects him to both the Al Sharqi family of Fujairah and the Al Maktoum family of Dubai, strengthening inter-emirate family ties within the UAE.

figures.name: Aisha bint Mohammed bin Hamad bin Mohammed Al Sharqi
figures.title: Sheikha
figures.roleDescription: Granddaughter of the ruler of Fujairah
figures.significance: Daughter of the Crown Prince, representing the next generation of the Al Sharqi dynasty.
figures.biography:
Sheikha Aisha bint Mohammed bin Hamad bin Mohammed Al Sharqi was born on November 1, 2011, as the eldest daughter of Crown Prince Mohammed bin Hamad bin Mohammed Al Sharqi and Sheikha Latifa bint Mohammed bin Rashid Al Maktoum. She is the granddaughter of the current ruler of Fujairah.

As a member of the younger generation of the Al Sharqi family, she represents the continuation of the dynasty and the strong family ties between the ruling families of Fujairah and Dubai through her mother's lineage.

figures.name: Fatima bint Mohammed bin Hamad bin Mohammed Al Sharqi
figures.title: Sheikha
figures.roleDescription: Granddaughter of the ruler of Fujairah
figures.significance: Daughter of the Crown Prince, representing the next generation of the Al Sharqi dynasty.
figures.biography:
Sheikha Fatima bint Mohammed bin Hamad bin Mohammed Al Sharqi was born on March 11, 2014, as a daughter of Crown Prince Mohammed bin Hamad bin Mohammed Al Sharqi and Sheikha Latifa bint Mohammed bin Rashid Al Maktoum. She is named after her great-grandmother, Sheikha Fatima bint Rashid Al Nuaimi.

As a member of the fourth generation of the modern Al Sharqi dynasty, she represents the future of the ruling family and maintains the strong inter-emirate connections through her maternal lineage to the Al Maktoum family of Dubai.

figures.name: Rashid bin Mohammed bin Hamad bin Mohammed Al Sharqi
figures.title: Sheikh
figures.roleDescription: Grandson of the ruler of Fujairah
figures.significance: Son of the Crown Prince, representing the next generation of the Al Sharqi dynasty.
figures.biography:
Sheikh Rashid bin Mohammed bin Hamad bin Mohammed Al Sharqi was born on December 15, 2015, as a son of Crown Prince Mohammed bin Hamad bin Mohammed Al Sharqi and Sheikha Latifa bint Mohammed bin Rashid Al Maktoum. He is the grandson of the current ruler of Fujairah.

His name honors the family tradition, being named after his uncle Sheikh Rashid bin Hamad bin Mohammed Al Sharqi. As a member of the youngest generation of the Al Sharqi family, he represents the continuation of the dynasty into the future.

figures.name: Hind bint Mohammed bin Hamad Al Sharqi
figures.title: Sheikha
figures.roleDescription: Granddaughter of the ruler of Fujairah
figures.significance: Youngest daughter of the Crown Prince, representing the newest generation of the Al Sharqi dynasty.
figures.biography:
Sheikha Hind bint Mohammed bin Hamad Al Sharqi was born on June 22, 2020, as the youngest daughter of Crown Prince Mohammed bin Hamad bin Mohammed Al Sharqi and Sheikha Latifa bint Mohammed bin Rashid Al Maktoum. She is the granddaughter of the current ruler of Fujairah.

As the youngest member of the immediate royal family mentioned in the records, she represents the newest generation of the Al Sharqi dynasty and the continuation of the strong family ties between Fujairah and Dubai through her maternal lineage.

figures.name: Ahmad al-Muhajir
figures.title: Founder of Ba 'Alawi lineage
figures.roleDescription: Ancestor who emigrated from Basra to Hadhramaut
figures.significance: Foundational ancestor of the Ba 'Alawi sada who emigrated from Basra to Hadhramaut in 931 (318H).
figures.biography:
Ahmad al-Muhajir is the foundational ancestor of the Ba 'Alawi sada, who emigrated from Basra to Hadhramaut in 931 (318H). He was a descendant of Muhammad through Husayn ibn Ali. When Ahmad al-Muhajir arrived in Hadhramaut, he maintained close family ties in Basra, where many of his relatives resided. His son Muhammad remained in Basra to oversee the family's property, together with his other sons, Ali and Hussein.

Al-Muhajir's children and grandchildren invested in Hadhramaut for many years derived from their holdings in Iraq. They continued to maintain contact with their ancestral homeland and cousins for a few generations, receiving news and accounts that helped preserve their shared heritage and historical memory. The Ba 'Alawi trace their lineage back to him, and classical scholars of Islam such as Ibn Hajar al-Haytami, Yusuf al-Nabhani and Murtada al-Zabidi have validated the genealogy of the Ba Alawi Sada.
achievements: Founded the Ba 'Alawi lineage in Hadhramaut
Established family presence outside Arabia

figures.name: Alawi bin 'Ubaydillah
figures.title: First descendant named 'Alawi
figures.roleDescription: First of al-Muhajir's descendants to be named 'Alawi
figures.significance: The first of al-Muhajir's descendants to be named 'Alawi, from whom the Ba 'Alawi take their name.
figures.biography:
Alawi bin 'Ubaydillah was the first of Ahmad al-Muhajir's descendants to be named 'Alawi, and from him the Ba 'Alawi family takes its name. He was the grandson of Ahmad al-Muhajir and the first Sayyid to be born in Hadhramaut. He was the only one of Imam al-Muhajir's descendants to produce a continued line in Hadhramaut, as the lines of his other grandsons in Hadhramaut—Basri (originally named Ismāʿīl) and Jadīd—ceased after a few generations.

Accordingly, Imam Al-Muhajir's descendants in Hadhramaut hold the name Bā 'Alawi or Bani 'Alawi ("descendants of Alawi") after him. The origin of the Ba 'Alawi name goes back to this ancestor, and the use of the name Ba 'Alawi came after they were influenced by the Hadhrami way of referring to their fathers, with the meaning of (Ba) among the Hadharem being "children of".
achievements: First descendant to bear the name 'Alawi
Established the continuing Ba 'Alawi line in Hadhramaut

figures.name: Muhammad bin Ali Baalawi
figures.title: Al-Faqih Al-Muqaddam
figures.roleDescription: Founder of the Ba 'Alawi Tariqa
figures.significance: Founded the Ba 'Alawi Tariqa and established the principle of non-violence for Alawi Sayyids.
figures.biography:
Muhammad bin Ali Baalawi, known as "Al-Faqih Al-Muqaddam" (The Foremost Jurist), was the founder of the Ba 'Alawi Tariqa, one of the Sufi orders spread in the Islamic world. During his time, Sayyid families in Hadhramaut were seen as a threat by other tribes, and due to regional instability, it was normal during his study that Muhammad bin Ali put a sword on his lap for protection.

Muhammad grew tired of the tension and bloodshed in the ranks of the believers, thus symbolically broke his sword and announced that his Tariqa and the way of Alawi Sayyids are non-violence and renounced any tariqa that uses violence. This established the peaceful nature of the Ba 'Alawi approach to spreading Islam, which would later influence the dissemination of Islam in Southeast Asia through traders and clerics of Hadhramaut who transited in India since the 15th century.
achievements: Founded the Ba 'Alawi Tariqa
Established the principle of non-violence for the Ba 'Alawi
Created peaceful approach to Islamic teaching

figures.name: Ali bin Alawi
figures.title: Khali' Qasam
figures.roleDescription: Descendant who established Tarim as Ba 'Alawi headquarters
figures.significance: Moved to Tarim in 521 AH and made it the headquarters of the Bani Alawi.
figures.biography:
Ali bin Alawi, known as "Khali' Qasam," was a descendant of Ahmad Al-Muhajir who played a crucial role in establishing the Ba 'Alawi presence in Tarim. In 521 AH, he moved to the city of Tarim and made it his home for himself and his children, following the family's earlier movements through various villages in the valley.

After Ahmad Al-Muhajir's descendants had moved through the valley's villages, settling in the village of Sumal for a time before moving to the village of Bayt Jubair, Ali bin Alawi's decision to settle in Tarim proved to be historically significant. Since his settlement there, Tarim has become the headquarters of the Bani Alawi to this day, serving as their spiritual and administrative center.
achievements: Established Tarim as the Ba 'Alawi headquarters
Consolidated Ba 'Alawi presence in a major city

figures.name: Al-Ḥabīb ʿUmar bin ʿAbd al-Raḥmān al-ʿAṭṭās
figures.nameAr: الحبيب عمر بن عبد الرحمن العطاس
figures.title: Al-Habib
figures.roleDescription: First Ba 'Alawi to be called Habib
figures.significance: The first person from the Bā ʿAlawī clan to be called Habib, establishing this honorific title.
figures.biography:
Al-Ḥabīb ʿUmar bin ʿAbd al-Raḥmān al-ʿAṭṭās (d. 1072 AH / 1661 CE) holds the distinction of being the first person from the Bā ʿAlawī clan to be called Habib. This represented an evolution in the titles used by Ba 'Alawi scholars and religious leaders over time.

Previously, descendants of Imam Ahmad al-Muhajir who became scholars in Islamic studies were called Imam, then Sheikh, but al-ʿAṭṭās was the first to be honored with the title Habib. This title became an important part of Ba 'Alawi tradition and continues to be used for respected religious leaders within the community.
achievements: First Ba 'Alawi to receive the title 'Habib'
Established new honorific tradition

figures.name: Muhammad 'Alawi al-Maliki
figures.title: Traditional Sunni Islamic Scholar
figures.roleDescription: Foremost traditional Sunni Islamic scholar from Saudi Arabia
figures.significance: Considered to be the Mujaddid of 20th-21st century and one of the foremost traditional Sunni Islamic scholars.
figures.biography:
Muhammad al-Hasan ibn Alawi ibn al-Abbas ibn Abd al-Aziz, also known as Muhammad ibn Alawi al-Maliki, was born in 1944 in Mecca and became one of the foremost traditional Sunni Islamic scholars of contemporary times. The Maliki family is one of the most respected families in Mecca and has produced great scholars who taught in the Haram of Mecca for centuries. Five of his ancestors had been the Maliki Imams of the Haram of Mecca.

With his father's instruction, he studied and mastered the various traditional Islamic sciences of Aqidah, Tafsir, Hadith, Seerah, Fiqh, Usul, Mustalah, Nahw, and received full Ijazah from scholars of Mecca and Medina to teach these sciences to others. In the early 1990s, the Government of Saudi Arabia began supporting practitioners of Sufism in the Hijaz region as a way to counter Wahhabism and bolster religious support of the state; al-Maliki became the self-imposed leader of Hijazi Sufism under state sponsorship, with several thousand supporters. He died in 2004 and was buried in Mecca, with Crown Prince 'Abd Allah stating that al-Maliki "was faithful both to his religion and country."
achievements: Mastered traditional Islamic sciences
Became leader of Hijazi Sufism
Authored numerous religious works
Received state recognition in Saudi Arabia

figures.name: Fadhil al-Milani
figures.title: Religious Scholar
figures.roleDescription: Prominent Shia scholar and father-in-law of Moustafa Al-Qazwini
figures.significance: Notable Shia religious scholar whose daughter married prominent Iraqi-American imam Moustafa Al-Qazwini.
figures.biography: Fadhil al-Milani was a respected Shia religious scholar who gained prominence in the religious community. He is mentioned in the sources as the father of a daughter who married Moustafa Al-Qazwini, an Iraqi-American Shia imam who founded several Islamic centers in California. The marriage connection suggests al-Milani held a position of respect within the Shia scholarly community, as such matrimonial alliances were typically formed between families of similar religious and social standing. His daughter's marriage to al-Qazwini, who went on to establish important Islamic institutions in the United States, indicates the al-Milani family's continued influence in Islamic scholarly circles.

figures.name: Hamda bint Hassan Al-Sulaiti
figures.title: Deputy Speaker of the Consultative Assembly
figures.roleDescription: Deputy Speaker of Qatar's Consultative Assembly
figures.significance: She serves as Deputy Speaker of Qatar's Consultative Assembly, representing the Al-Sulaiti family in high-level government positions.
figures.biography:
Hamda bint Hassan Al-Sulaiti is an appointed member of Qatar's Consultative Assembly and currently serves as Deputy Speaker of the council. She holds one of the key leadership positions in Qatar's legislative body, which consists of 45 members who are directly appointed by the Emir.

As Deputy Speaker, she plays an important role in the functioning of Qatar's legislative process, working alongside the Chairman Hassan bin Abdullah Al-Ghanim. Her position demonstrates the Al-Sulaiti family's continued prominence in Qatar's political establishment and their involvement in the country's governance structures.
achievements: Appointed as Deputy Speaker of Qatar's Consultative Assembly

figures.name: Ghanem Al Sulaiti
figures.title: Playwright and Actor
figures.roleDescription: Prominent Qatari playwright, actor, and theatrical producer
figures.significance: He is a leading figure in Qatar's theatre scene, best known for his politically themed plays and contributions to Gulf cultural arts.
figures.biography:
Ghanem Al Sulaiti was born on 1 January 1956 in Manama, Bahrain, to a mixed family background with a Qatari father, Emirati mother, and Saudi grandmother. He lived in Bahrain during part of his childhood before his family moved to Qatar. In 1978, he graduated from Egypt's Higher Institute of Dramatic Arts.

Al Sulaiti began his theatrical career early, participating in his first play 'The Garment Looks the Best with a Patch from its Own Material' in 1971 while at Dar Almualimeen. He helped form Qatar's first official theatre group, the Qatari Theatrical Troupe, in 1972, and later established The Student Acting Band in 1973. His breakthrough came with the 1985 play 'The Fighters' (Al Mutarishqun), which won him Best Actor and Best Theatrical Art awards at the Carthage Theatre Festival in Tunisia.

In 1986, Al Sulaiti founded Qatar's first theatrical production company and wrote 'Antar and Abla,' the first Qatari play to run continuously for a month and be distributed on VHS. He has been active in television, notably starring in the series 'What's Happening?' in 2018, which satirized the Qatar blockade. He won the Sharjah Award for Arab Theater Innovation in 2016, cementing his status as a major cultural figure in the Gulf region.
achievements: Founded Qatar's first theatrical production company in 1986
Won Best Actor Award at Carthage Theatre Festival 1985
Won Best Theatrical Art Award at Carthage Theatre Festival 1985
Won Sharjah Award for Arab Theater Innovation in 2016
Created first Qatari play to run continuously for a month

figures.name: Hamad Almuhannadi
figures.title: Former Member of Parliament
figures.roleDescription: Former Member of Parliament in Bahrain
figures.significance: Notable member of the Almuhannada family who served as a Member of Parliament in Bahrain.
figures.biography:
Hamad Almuhannadi is a member of the Almuhannada family, which is described as an offshoot of the Hajri families that are part of the broader Qahtan tribal confederation. The Almuhannada family is noted for taking a public role in Bahrain, despite the country housing the smallest number of Qahtanis compared to other Gulf countries.

Almuhannadi served as a Member of Parliament in Bahrain, representing his family's continued involvement in public affairs. His service in parliament demonstrates the political engagement of the Almuhannada family within Bahraini society, continuing a tradition of public service among the Hajri-affiliated families in the country.
achievements: Served as Member of Parliament in Bahrain

figures.name: Shansab
figures.title: Founder
figures.roleDescription: Legendary founder of the Ghurid dynasty
figures.significance: Established the Ghurid dynasty according to traditional accounts.
figures.biography:
According to Minhaj-i-Siraj in Tabaqat-i-Nasiri, Shansab established the Ghurid dynasty. The account claims he was converted by the Arab Caliph Ali, though this is dismissed as unlikely by historians Mohammad Habib and K. A. Nizami. The early history of Ghor and the Ghurid origins remain unclear, with various legendary accounts providing different versions of the dynasty's founding.

The Ghurid family was known by the actual name Āl-e Šansab (Persianized: Šansabānī), which is the Arabic pronunciation of the originally Middle Persian name Wišnasp. The dynasty originated from the Ghor region in present-day central Afghanistan, where they initially started out as local chiefs before gradually converting to Sunni Islam and expanding their territorial control.

figures.name: Amir Banji
figures.title: Ghurid Prince
figures.roleDescription: Early Ghurid ruler whose rule was legitimized by the Abbasids
figures.significance: His rule was legitimized by the Abbasid caliph Harun al-Rashid, establishing Ghurid authority.
figures.biography:
Amir Banji was a certain Ghurid prince who was the ruler of Ghor and ancestor of the medieval Ghurid rulers. His rule was legitimized by the Abbasid caliph Harun al-Rashid, which provided important political validation for the Ghurid dynasty's authority in the region.

According to Minhaj-i-Siraj, the Ghurid Amir Faulad, who was presumably connected to this lineage, assisted Abu Muslim in overthrowing the Umayyads during the Abbasid Revolution. A legend recounts a dispute between two prominent families of the Ghor area, and they sought the intercession of the Abbasids, with the ancestor of the Shansabi family being subsequently confirmed as the ruler by Harun al-Rashid.
achievements: Legitimized Ghurid rule through Abbasid recognition

figures.name: Amir Suri
figures.title: King of the Ghurid dynasty
figures.roleDescription: 9th-10th century Ghurid ruler who fought against the Saffarids
figures.significance: Known for resisting the Saffarid conquest and maintaining Ghurid independence in the region.
figures.biography:
Amīr Sūrī was the king of the Ghurid dynasty from the 9th-century to the 10th-century. According to some legends, he was a descendant of the Ghurid king Amir Banji, whose rule was legitimized by the Abbasid caliph Harun al-Rashid. Amir Suri is known to have fought the Saffarid ruler Ya'qub ibn al-Layth al-Saffar, who managed to conquer much of Khurasan except Ghur.

Amir Suri was later succeeded by his son Muhammad ibn Suri. Although Amir Suri bore an Arabic title and his son had an Islamic name, they followed Buddhism and were considered pagans by the surrounding Muslim people. It was only during the reign of Muhammad's son Abu Ali ibn Muhammad that the Ghurid dynasty became an Islamic dynasty. The Ghurids originated from the Ghuristan mountains, and were divided into numerous tribes, among which, the Shansabani tribe had the most authority.
achievements: Successfully resisted Saffarid conquest of Ghor
Maintained Ghurid independence against Ya'qub ibn al-Layth al-Saffar

figures.name: Abu Ali ibn Muhammad
figures.title: Ghurid Ruler
figures.roleDescription: First Muslim ruler of the Ghurid dynasty
figures.significance: Converted the Ghurid dynasty to Islam, ending their Buddhist period.
figures.biography:
Abu Ali ibn Muhammad was the grandson of Amir Suri and son of Muhammad ibn Suri. He represents a crucial turning point in Ghurid history as the first ruler to convert the dynasty to Islam. Before his reign, the Ghurids followed Buddhism and were considered pagans by the surrounding Muslim people, despite bearing Arabic titles and Islamic names.

According to historical accounts, the early Ghurids followed Paganism before being converted to Islam by Abu Ali ibn Muhammad in 1149. His conversion marked the transformation of the Ghurid dynasty from a Buddhist principality to an Islamic one, which would later enable their expansion and integration into the broader Islamic world. Under his influence, Islam was firmly established among the Ghurid ruling class.
achievements: Converted the Ghurid dynasty to Islam
Established Islamic rule in Ghor

figures.name: Qutb al-Din Muhammad
figures.title: Ghurid Leader
figures.roleDescription: Local Ghurid leader who was poisoned by the Ghaznavids
figures.significance: His death sparked the Ghurid-Ghaznavid conflict that led to Ghurid independence.
figures.biography:
Qutb al-Din Muhammad was a local Ghurid leader in the mid-12th century during the period when the Ghurids were still bound to the Ghaznavids and Seljuks. In 1149, he had taken refuge in the city of Ghazni after having a quarrel with his brother Sayf al-Din Suri. However, the Ghaznavid ruler Bahram-Shah of Ghazna poisoned him while he was seeking sanctuary.

His death became the catalyst for the Ghurid war of independence. In revenge for his brother's murder, Sayf marched towards Ghazni and defeated Bahram-Shah, though Sayf was later captured and crucified at Pul-i Yak Taq when Bahram returned with reinforcements. This cycle of revenge ultimately led to Ala al-Din Husayn's devastating sack of Ghazni, earning him the title 'Jahānsūz' (the world burner), and marked the beginning of serious Ghurid resistance to Ghaznavid authority.

figures.name: Sayf al-Din Suri
figures.title: Ghurid Leader
figures.roleDescription: Brother of Qutb al-Din Muhammad who sought to avenge his death
figures.significance: Led the first major Ghurid military response against the Ghaznavids in revenge for his brother's murder.
figures.biography:
Sayf al-Din Suri was the brother of Qutb al-Din Muhammad and played a crucial role in the early Ghurid resistance against the Ghaznavids. When his brother was poisoned by the Ghaznavid ruler Bahram-Shah of Ghazna in 1149, Sayf immediately set out to seek revenge. He marched towards Ghazni and managed to defeat Bahram-Shah in the initial confrontation.

However, one year later in 1150, Bahram-Shah returned with reinforcements and scored a decisive victory against Sayf. Following his defeat, Sayf was captured and met a brutal end - he was crucified at Pul-i Yak Taq. His death, along with that of his brother, further fueled the cycle of revenge that would eventually lead to Ala al-Din Husayn's devastating retaliation against the Ghaznavids. The brothers' deaths became rallying points for Ghurid resistance and independence movements.
achievements: Initially defeated Bahram-Shah of Ghazna in revenge for his brother

figures.name: Ala al-Din Husayn
figures.title: Ghurid King
figures.roleDescription: Ghurid ruler known as 'Jahānsūz' (the world burner)
figures.significance: Sacked Ghazni and established Ghurid independence, earning the title 'the world burner.'
figures.biography:
Ala al-Din Husayn (1149-61) was one of the youngest brothers of Sayf al-Din Suri and became the newly crowned Ghurid king following the deaths of his two brothers. Like his predecessors, he set out to avenge their murders by the Ghaznavids. He managed to defeat Bahram-Shah decisively and then had the city of Ghazni completely sacked - the city burned for seven days and seven nights. He also sacked the Ghaznavid fortresses and palaces of Bost. These devastating actions earned him the title of Jahānsūz, meaning 'the world burner.'

In 1152, Ala al-Din Husayn refused to pay tribute to the Seljuks and marched an army from Firozkoh, but was defeated and captured at Nab in the Harīrūd Valley by Sultan Ahmed Sanjar after his forces defected to the Seljuqs. He remained a prisoner for two years until released for a heavy ransom. After Sanjar was captured by Ghuzz nomads in 1153, Husayn expanded Ghurid domains, conquering Garchistan, Tukharistan, Zamindawar, Bust, Bamiyan and other parts of Khurasan before his death in 1161.
achievements: Sacked Ghazni and destroyed Ghaznavid power
Conquered multiple regions including Bamiyan and parts of Khurasan
Established Ghurid independence from both Ghaznavids and Seljuks

figures.name: Ghiyath al-Din Muhammad
figures.title: Ghurid Sultan
figures.roleDescription: Senior partner in the Ghurid dyarchy who controlled the western empire
figures.significance: Co-ruler who expanded the Ghurid Empire to its greatest extent, controlling western territories from Fīrōzkōh.
figures.biography:
Ghiyath al-Din Muhammad was the son of Baha al-Din Sam I and cousin of Sayf al-Din Muhammad. He succeeded to the Ghurid throne and proved himself to be a capable king. Right after his ascension, he, with the aid of his loyal brother Muhammad of Ghor (later known as 'Shihabuddin Ghuri'), killed a rival Ghurid chief named Abu'l Abbas. He then defeated his uncle Fakhr al-Din Masud who claimed the Ghurid throne and had allied with the Seljuq governor of Herat and Balkh.

Ghiyath established a dyarchy with his brother Muhammad of Ghor, where Ghiyath governed the western part of the empire from his capital at Fīrōzkōh while Muhammad controlled the eastern territories including the Indian conquests. During their joint rule, the Ghurid empire reached its greatest territorial extent. Ghiyath was particularly occupied with expansion in the west, engaging in a protracted conflict with the Khwarezmian Empire and reaching as far as Gorgan on the Caspian Sea. He died on 13 March 1203 of illness caused by rheumatic disorders, after which Muhammad became sole ruler of the vast Ghurid Empire.
achievements: Established the successful Ghurid dyarchy
Expanded Ghurid control to the Caspian Sea region
Defeated multiple rival claimants to secure Ghurid unity

figures.name: Muhammad of Ghor
figures.title: Ghurid Sultan
figures.roleDescription: Conqueror of northern India and co-ruler of the Ghurid Empire
figures.significance: Conquered northern India and established the foundations of Muslim rule in the Indian subcontinent.
figures.biography:
Muhammad of Ghor, also known as Shihabuddin Ghuri, was the younger brother of Ghiyath al-Din Muhammad and served as the junior partner in their dyarchy. While Ghiyath controlled the western territories from Fīrōzkōh, Muhammad was responsible for eastern expansion, particularly into the Indian subcontinent. In 1173, after multiple attempts, he reconquered Ghazni from the Ghuzz Turks. In 1175, he began his systematic conquest of northern India, initially crossing the Indus River through the Gomal Pass.

Muhammad's Indian campaigns were largely successful despite an early defeat in Gujarat at the Battle of Kasahrada in 1178. He conquered key territories including Multan, Sindh, and Peshawar, and eliminated the last Ghaznavid principality in Punjab by capturing Lahore in 1186. His most famous victories came against the Rajput confederacy led by Prithviraja III - after losing the First Battle of Tarain in 1191, he returned with Turkish mounted archers and won decisively in the Second Battle of Tarain in 1192. His generals, particularly Qutb ud-Din Aibak, conquered Delhi in 1193 and pushed eastward, defeating Jayachandra at the Battle of Chandawar in 1194. Muhammad was assassinated near Jhelum in March 1206 by Ismāʿīlīs, ending the unified Ghurid Empire.
achievements: Conquered northern India and established Muslim rule
Defeated the Rajput confederacy at the Second Battle of Tarain
Established the foundations of the Delhi Sultanate
Eliminated the last Ghaznavid stronghold in Lahore

figures.name: Sheikh Saeed bin Hamad Al Qasimi
figures.title: Ruler of Kalba
figures.roleDescription: Founding patriarch of the House of Sheikh Saeed bin Hamad Al Qasimi
figures.significance: Built the administrative center for the Emirate of Kalba and its subordinate areas from 1903 until 1937.
figures.biography: Sheikh Saeed bin Hamad Al Qasimi was the ruler of Kalba who established the administrative center that would serve his emirate from 1903 until 1937. He built his house between 1898 and 1901 directly across from the historical fort in Kalba. The house served as the administrative center for the Emirate of Kalba and its subordinate areas throughout his reign. The structure featured seventeen rooms, three chambers, a yard, and a visitors' reception room, where Al Qasimi concluded numerous significant treaties and agreements with the British during their occupation of the region. After his death in 1937, the house continued to serve administrative functions until it was later converted into a museum in 1999.
achievements: Established administrative center for Emirate of Kalba
Built house that served as seat of government from 1903-1937
Concluded significant treaties with British authorities

figures.name: Nancy Khedouri
figures.title: Politician, Businesswoman and Author
figures.roleDescription: Member of the National Assembly of Bahrain since 2010
figures.significance: First Jewish woman to serve in the Bahraini National Assembly and documented the history of Bahraini Jews.
figures.biography:
Nancy Dinah Elly Khedouri was born in 1975 into one of the Jewish families in Bahrain, a family of importers of tablecloths and linens. Her direct family is third generation in Bahrain, and she is the cousin of Houda Nonoo, the Bahraini Ambassador to the United States from 2008 to 2013. In 2007, she documented the history of Bahraini Jews from the first settlers (late 1880s) to present days in her book 'From Our Beginning to Present Day'.

In 2010, Nancy Khedouri became a member of the National Assembly of Bahrain, making significant contributions to Bahraini politics. She has worked on smoothening relationships with Israel by appearing publicly with political figures such as Yisrael Katz. In April 2017, she represented her country at the World Jewish Congress. In November 2010, she was appointed to replace Houda Nonoo in Parliament after Nonoo became ambassador to the United States.
achievements: First Jewish woman member of Bahrain's National Assembly
Author of 'From Our Beginning to Present Day' documenting Bahraini Jewish history
Represented Bahrain at the World Jewish Congress in 2017

figures.name: Rose Tarlow
figures.title: Interior Designer
figures.roleDescription: Interior designer, furniture and textile designer, and author
figures.significance: Renowned interior designer known for creating elegant residences for notable clients and author of design books.
figures.biography:
Rose Tarlow (née Khedouri) is an interior designer, furniture and textile designer, and author based in Los Angeles, California. She graduated from Emerson College in 1960 with a Bachelor of Science in Theater Arts and married in 1961. After attending classes at the New York School of Interior Design and the Parsons School of Design, she established an interior design shop in Englewood, New Jersey. In 1971, having divorced and moved to California, she married lawyer Barry Tarlow.

She established Rose K. Tarlow Antiques. Ltd. in 1974, and Rose Tarlow Melrose House in 1981. Tarlow is known for creating rooms with highly refined wood, plaster, and stone finishes, furnished with antiques, and infused with a personal blend of minimalism and romanticism. Her notable projects include residences for Barbara Walters, David Geffen, and the Eli and Edythe Broad residence designed with Frank Gehry. She has authored books including 'The Private House' (2001) and 'Three Houses' (2022).
achievements: Established successful interior design businesses
Designed notable residences for prominent clients including Barbara Walters and David Geffen
Author of acclaimed design books

figures.name: Ezra Zilkha
figures.title: American Financier
figures.roleDescription: Prominent banker and financier from the Zilkha banking family
figures.significance: Member of the prominent Zilkha banking family that rebuilt their financial empire after nationalization in the Middle East.
figures.biography:
Ezra Zilkha (1925–2019) was an American financier and member of the prominent Zilkha banking family. The Zilkha family were bankers from the Middle East, with the patriarch Khedouri Zilkha establishing their bank in 1899. The family faced significant challenges when their assets were nationalized in three countries from 1950 to 1956 (Iraq, Syria and Egypt), forcing them to rebuild their financial empire in the US and Europe.

Ezra Zilkha was the father of Donald Zilkha, who became a successful American financier and businessman. The family's resilience and ability to rebuild their banking operations after losing their Middle Eastern assets demonstrates their business acumen and adaptability. Ezra's legacy continued through his son's successful career in finance and investment.
achievements: Rebuilt family banking operations after Middle Eastern nationalizations
Established successful financial career in the United States

figures.name: Khedouri Zilkha
figures.title: Iraqi Banker
figures.roleDescription: Founder and patriarch of the Zilkha banking dynasty
figures.significance: Founded the Zilkha bank in 1899, establishing one of the Middle East's prominent banking dynasties.
figures.biography:
Khedouri Zilkha (1884–1956) was an Iraqi banker who established the foundation of what would become one of the Middle East's most significant banking dynasties. In 1899, he founded the Zilkha bank, which would grow to become a major financial institution in the region. The bank operated successfully across multiple Middle Eastern countries for several decades.

However, the family faced severe challenges during the mid-20th century when their banking operations were nationalized by governments in Iraq, Syria, and Egypt between 1950 and 1956. This forced the family to rebuild their financial empire in the United States and Europe. Despite these setbacks, Khedouri Zilkha's legacy lived on through his descendants, who successfully reestablished the family's prominence in international banking and finance, particularly in the United States.
achievements: Founded the Zilkha bank in 1899
Established a major Middle Eastern banking dynasty
Built successful banking operations across multiple countries

figures.name: Donald Zilkha
figures.title: American Financier and Businessman
figures.roleDescription: Founder of multiple investment companies and business ventures
figures.significance: Successful financier who rebuilt the family's business empire in America after Middle Eastern nationalizations.
figures.biography:
Donald Elias Zilkha was born on April 8, 1951, in New York as the son of Ezra Zilkha. The Zilkha family were bankers from the Middle East whose patriarch Khedouri Zilkha established their bank in 1899, but the family was nationalized in three countries from 1950 to 1956 (Iraq, Syria and Egypt) and had to rebuild in the US and Europe. Donald attended Lycee Francais de New York before attending The Hill School, then graduated from Wesleyan University's College of Social Studies in 1973.
