figures.name: Ahmed al-Rajhi
figures.title: Minister of Labor, Saudi Arabia
figures.roleDescription: Saudi government minister and businessman
figures.significance: Appointed as Saudi Arabia's Labor Minister in 2018, representing growing private sector expertise in government.
figures.biography: Ahmed al-Rajhi is a businessman who was appointed as Saudi Arabia's Labor Minister in June 2018 by King Salman as part of government reforms. His appointment signaled a growing role for private sector expertise in the Saudi government, reflecting the kingdom's efforts to diversify its economy and implement Vision 2030 reforms. The appointment was part of broader government changes that also included the establishment of the Ministry of Culture and the Council of Royal Reserves.
achievements: Appointed as Saudi Arabia's Labor Minister in 2018
Represents private sector expertise in Saudi government

figures.name: Abdullah Mohammed Ali Al-Dabbagh
figures.title: Former Minister of Agriculture
figures.roleDescription: Saudi government minister and business patriarch
figures.significance: Former Saudi Minister of Agriculture who founded the Al-Dabbagh Group business conglomerate in 1962.
figures.biography:
Abdullah Mohammed Ali Al-Dabbagh served as the Minister of Agriculture of Saudi Arabia and was a prominent figure in the Saudi business community. In 1962, he founded the Al-Dabbagh Group (ADG), establishing what would become a major family business conglomerate in Saudi Arabia. His entrepreneurial vision laid the foundation for one of the country's significant business empires.

As a government minister, Abdullah Al-Dabbagh played an important role in Saudi Arabia's agricultural sector development. His dual role as both a public servant and private entrepreneur exemplified the close relationship between government service and business development in the Kingdom during the mid-20th century. The business empire he established would later be inherited and expanded by his son Amr Al-Dabbagh.
achievements: Served as Saudi Minister of Agriculture
Founded Al-Dabbagh Group business conglomerate in 1962

figures.name: Amr Al-Dabbagh
figures.title: Chairman and CEO of Al-Dabbagh Group
figures.roleDescription: Saudi businessman and former head of SAGIA
figures.significance: Headed the Saudi Arabian General Investment Authority (SAGIA) from 2004 to 2012 and leads the Al-Dabbagh Group business conglomerate.
figures.biography:
Amr Al-Dabbagh was born in 1966 to Abdullah Al-Dabbagh, the former Saudi Minister for Agriculture. He obtained his Bachelor of Business Administration from King Abdulaziz University and went on to become a prominent figure in Saudi business and government circles. As chairman and CEO of Al-Dabbagh Group (ADG), he inherited and expanded the family business conglomerate that his father founded in 1962.

From 2004 to 2012, Al-Dabbagh served as governor of the Saudi Arabian General Investment Authority (SAGIA), playing a crucial role in promoting foreign investment in Saudi Arabia. He was the founding Chairman of the think tank the Jeddah Economic Forum and served as a board member of the Jeddah Chamber of Commerce & Industry. His philanthropic work included creating Philanthropy University in 2015, which offers Massive Open Online Courses (MOOCs) to Global South non-profit leaders, and founding the UK-based Stars Foundation that operated from 2001-2020.

In November 2017, Al-Dabbagh was detained as part of a wide-ranging anti-corruption purge that also involved prominent Saudi figures including Prince Alwaleed bin Talal. He was held at the Ritz Carlton hotel in Riyadh, which had been converted into a makeshift prison. Despite the detention and allegations, no specific charges were ever filed against him, and he was released without charge on 23 January 2019, continuing to deny all allegations against him.
achievements: Headed Saudi Arabian General Investment Authority (SAGIA) from 2004 to 2012
Founded the Jeddah Economic Forum think tank
Created Philanthropy University in 2015
Founded the UK-based Stars Foundation (2001-2020)
Board member of Jeddah Chamber of Commerce & Industry

figures.name: Sheikh Abdullah Al Turki
figures.title: Secretary-General of the World Muslim League
figures.roleDescription: Religious scholar and leader of international Islamic organization
figures.significance: Served as secretary-general of the World Muslim League and was a member of the Council of Senior Ulema.
figures.biography:
Sheikh Abdullah Al Turki served as secretary-general of the World Muslim League and was a member of the Council of Senior Ulema. He was directly challenged by Prince Turki bin Faisal Al Saud after the 9/11 attacks regarding the proper role of religious scholars versus rulers in matters of state.

Prince Turki bin Faisal argued against Sheikh Al Turki's position, stating that 'those responsible for affairs of state are the rulers,' whereas religious scholars 'only act in an advisory capacity.' This public disagreement highlighted tensions within Saudi religious and political establishments following the September 11 attacks.

figures.name: Aisha al-Mana
figures.title: Director and Activist
figures.roleDescription: Saudi activist, feminist, and healthcare executive
figures.significance: Pioneering Saudi women's rights activist and one of the first Saudi women to earn a PhD, who became the first female hospital director in Saudi Arabia.
figures.biography:
Aisha al-Mana was born in 1948 in the Saudi city of Khobar. After studying the Quran at Al-Kutab in Saudi Arabia, she traveled to Egypt where she finished school. She then studied sociology at the American University of Beirut and obtained a bachelor's degree in sociology from the University of Oregon in 1971, followed by a master's degree in sociology from Arizona State University. She obtained her PhD from the University of Colorado Boulder in 1982, becoming one of the first Saudi women to do so, with a dissertation titled 'Economic Development and its Impact on the Status of Women in Saudi Arabia'.

In 1985, al-Mana established Al-Sharika Al-Khalijiah Lil Inmaa (Al-Khalijiah Development Company), the first company in Saudi Arabia run completely by women, offering computer training and technical education to women. She became the first female hospital director in Saudi Arabia in 1990 as Director of Support Services at the Almana Group of Hospitals. Al-Mana participated in three major campaigns protesting the driving ban on women (1990, 2011, and 2013) and was one of forty-six women who organized a demonstration in Riyadh on November 6, 1990. She has established foundations to support the education of Saudi women, including scholarship programs at the University of Oregon and endowments for women in nursing and health sciences. In May 2018, she was arrested along with other female Saudi activists but was released after a few days.
achievements: One of the first Saudi women to earn a PhD (1982)
Established first company in Saudi Arabia run completely by women (1985)
First female hospital director in Saudi Arabia (1990)
Led women's driving rights demonstrations (1990, 2011, 2013)
Established scholarship programs for Saudi women's education

figures.name: Mona Yousuf Almoayyed
figures.title: Managing Director
figures.roleDescription: Managing Director of Y. K. Almoayyed & Sons
figures.significance: Voted the third most influential Arab woman in the MENA region by Forbes Middle East in 2013 and 2018, leading one of Bahrain's oldest conglomerates.
figures.biography:
Mona Yousuf Almoayyed is a businesswoman, philanthropist, environmentalist and women's rights activist who serves as the managing director of one of Bahrain's oldest conglomerates, Y. K. Almoayyed & Sons. A Bachelor of Business Administration, Mona has been instrumental in overseeing the business endeavours of Y. K. Almoayyed & Sons, leading it meticulously towards its desired destination.

Voted the third most influential Arab Women's List in the MENA region by Forbes Middle East in 2013, and in 2018, Mona is a member of the board of directors for BMMI and Ebda Bank besides other companies. She is well known for her charity work and frequently speaks about the role of women in building a better society today. Mona has been a firm believer of 'Business with a Conscience' principle and is involved in many charitable institutions. Besides her Business and Social Activity, Mona strives to draw a perfect balance between being a mother, housewife and mentor role she plays. Having been brought up in a conservative environment in the 70s Middle East, the young girl's childhood was simple and disciplined like any middle class Arab girl's upbringing.
achievements: Voted third most influential Arab woman in MENA region by Forbes Middle East (2013, 2018)
Managing Director of Y. K. Almoayyed & Sons
Board member of BMMI and Ebda Bank
Prominent philanthropist and women's rights activist

figures.name: Muhammad ibn Ali al-Jawad
figures.title: Ninth Imam of Twelver Shia
figures.roleDescription: Religious leader and descendant of Prophet Muhammad
figures.significance: Ninth of the Twelve Imams in Shia Islam, known for his religious teachings and correspondence with followers on Islamic law.
figures.biography: Muhammad ibn Ali al-Jawad was born in Medina around 8 April 811 as the son of Ali al-Rida, the eighth Imam. He became Imam at the young age of seven after his father's death in 818, which initially caused controversy among Shia communities due to his youth. Despite this, most Imamite Shias accepted his imamate, believing he received perfect religious knowledge through divine inspiration. In 830, he was summoned to Baghdad by Caliph al-Ma'mun and married to the caliph's daughter Umm al-Fadl, though this marriage remained childless. He organized Shia community affairs through an extensive network of representatives (wokala) across the Abbasid empire. In 835, he was again summoned to Baghdad by Caliph al-Mu'tasim and died there at age 25. Shia sources unanimously claim he was poisoned by his wife at the instigation of al-Mu'tasim, while Sunni sources remain silent about the cause of his death.
achievements: Maintained extensive network of representatives across Abbasid empire
Preserved numerous religious teachings and correspondence on Islamic law
Successfully consolidated imamate despite young age

figures.name: Ali ibn Muhammad al-Hadi
figures.title: Tenth Imam of Twelver Shia
figures.roleDescription: Religious leader and son of Muhammad al-Jawad
figures.significance: Tenth of the Twelve Imams who was held under surveillance in Samarra for twenty years, marking the end of direct Imam leadership of the Shia community.
figures.biography: Ali ibn Muhammad al-Hadi was born on 7 March 828 in Sorayya, a village near Medina, as the son of Muhammad al-Jawad and Samana, a freed slave of North African origin. He became Imam at age seven after his father's death in 835. Around 848, he was summoned from Medina to the Abbasid capital Samarra by Caliph al-Mutawakkil, known for his anti-Shia policies. There he lived under constant surveillance for twenty years until his death, unable to freely meet with ordinary Shias. Despite restrictions, he maintained communication with an underground network of representatives who organized Shia community affairs. He endured numerous attempts by Abbasid court members to humiliate him, house searches, temporary imprisonments, and alleged murder plots. He died on 21 June 868 during the caliphate of al-Mu'tazz, with most Shia sources claiming he was poisoned by the Abbasids. His restricted life in Samarra effectively ended the direct leadership of the Shia community by the Imams.
achievements: Maintained underground network of representatives despite surveillance
Endured twenty years of persecution while preserving Shia community organization
Authored theological treatise on free will and various religious texts

figures.name: Sheikh Mirza Jawad Kubar Tabrizi
figures.title: Grand Ayatollah
figures.roleDescription: Iranian Shia marja and religious authority
figures.significance: Prominent Iranian Shia religious authority and student of Grand Ayatollah al-Khoei, known for his scholarship and mourning rituals advocacy.
figures.biography: Sheikh Mirza Jawad Kubar Tabrizi was born in Tabriz in 1926 to Ali Kubar, a merchant, and Fatima Sultan, claiming descent from Prophet Muhammad through his mother. He initially pursued secular education but changed to Islamic seminary studies despite family opposition due to Pahlavi government pressure on clerics. He traveled to Qom in 1948 to study under Grand Ayatollah Borujerdi, then moved to Najaf in 1953 where he became a close student of Sayyid Abu Al-Qasim al-Khoei for nearly twenty years. He obtained the level of ijtihad and specialized in philosophy, Quranic exegesis, and rijal. In 1976, he was deported by the Baathist regime and returned to Iran, settling in Qom where he resumed teaching. After al-Khoei's death in 1992, many followers turned to him as their marja. He was known for his passionate advocacy of mourning rituals for Imam Husayn and his strong positions on religious matters. He died on November 20, 2006, and was buried in the Fatima Masumeh Shrine in Qom.
achievements: Became recognized marja after al-Khoei's death
Authored numerous volumes on jurisprudence and principles
Led large annual mourning processions in Qom
Issued statements on international religious freedom issues

figures.name: Jawad Ahmad
figures.title: Pakistani Pop Singer and Politician
figures.roleDescription: Musician turned politician and social activist
figures.significance: Pakistani pop singer who transitioned to politics and founded the leftist Barabri Party Pakistan while maintaining social work initiatives.
figures.biography: Jawad Ahmad was born on 29 September 1970 in Lahore into a Kashmiri family, with both parents being college professors teaching Political Science. He received a degree in Mechanical Engineering from the University of Engineering and Technology (UET), Lahore, where he participated in musical and literary societies. While at university, he became a member of the pop band Jupiters alongside Ali Azmat, before launching a solo career. He gained fame with his song 'Allah Meray Dil Kay Ander,' showcasing his Sufist inclinations, and released three solo albums plus several drama OSTs. In 2002, he was appointed as Pakistan's ambassador for polio eradication by the Ministry of Health and UNICEF. In May 2017, he launched his leftist Barabri political party, fielding 14 candidates in the 2018 elections though winning no seats. He runs ten schools in Pakistan's least developed areas under the Taleem for All program and continues his social work alongside his political activities.
achievements: Released three successful solo albums
Appointed Pakistan's polio eradication ambassador
Founded Barabri political party
Established ten schools for underprivileged areas
Created awareness campaigns against drug addiction

figures.name: Allama Syed Jawad Naqvi
figures.title: Islamic Scholar and Religious Leader
figures.roleDescription: Pakistani Shia theorist and founder of Islamic educational institutions
figures.significance: Leading Pakistani Shia theorist who founded multiple Islamic seminaries and promotes theories of Wilayat-i Faqih, often called the representative of the Wilayat in Pakistan.
figures.biography: Allama Syed Jawad Naqvi was born on 1 February 1963 in Thipra Syedan village in Haripur Hazara District, Pakistan. He studied religious philosophy, theology, and Shia jurisprudence in Islamic seminaries in Pakistan and Iran, with notable teachers including Abdollah Javadi-Amoli and Mohammad-Taqi Mesbah-Yazdi. He founded and serves as chancellor of Jamia Urwat-ul-Wusqa in Lahore and many other Islamic schools, including Jamia Jaffria in Gujranwala and Jamia Ummul Kitaab in Lahore. He also heads the Deen-ul-Qayyim Online Islamic school and Siraat Education School System. Naqvi is the editor of the monthly religious magazine Masharab-e-Naab and is known for his dialogue-filled sermons. He is a staunch supporter of Iran's Islamic Revolution and propagates hard-line versions of Wilayat-i Faqih. In 2012-2013, he led the Islamic Awakening Movement condemning blasphemy of Muhammad. He has authored several works in Urdu on Islamic topics and continues to be a influential voice in Pakistani Shia communities.
achievements: Founded multiple Islamic seminaries including Jamia Urwat-ul-Wusqa
Established monthly magazine Masharab-e-Naab
Led Islamic Awakening Movement in 2012-2013
Authored several books on Islam including 'Islam and Secularism'

figures.name: Mohammed bin Said bin Asalm Al Shanfari
figures.title: Dramatist, Playwright and Director
figures.roleDescription: Pioneer of Omani theatre and drama
figures.significance: Established the first official national theatre group in Oman and was instrumental in developing Omani dramatic arts.
figures.biography:
Mohammed bin Said bin Asalm Al Shanfari was born on 26 December 1949 in Salalah, Dhofar, Oman, where he was raised with his two brothers Ahmed and Abdullah. His mother was Saida Bint Abdulazis Al Ruwas and his father was Sheikh Said Bin Asalm Mohammed Al Shanfari. After early education at home and at Al Saidiya School in Salalah, he attended an English-curriculum Oil-company school in Abu Dhabi. He obtained advanced degrees including a master's degree in Theatrical Directing from the University of Sofia, Bulgaria (1974), a Higher Diploma in Theatre Studies from Cardiff University, UK (1978), and a Television Sciences Higher Diploma from Syracuse University, New York (1980).

Mohammed Al-Shanfari began his professional career in 1975 as Head of the Theatre Section at the Ministry of Information and Culture, where he established the first official national theatre group in Oman, known today as The Youth Theatre. He later served as Director of Local Programs and Director of Play-writing at Sultanate of Oman Television (1978-1981), Director of the Oman Youth Theatre (1981-1992), and Arts Specialist in various governmental cultural organizations until his retirement in December 1996. Throughout his career, he produced and directed numerous plays and was instrumental in developing Omani theatre arts, earning recognition as someone whose 'ideas inspired a generation.'

He made significant contributions to Gulf theatre culture, participating in international festivals and serving on cultural committees. He received multiple awards including the Gold Medal for senior members of the theatre movement in the Gulf (1988), Oman Gold Medals (1990, 1993), and posthumous lifetime achievement awards from the Arab Artist Society (2016) and the Oman Society for Theatre (2017). He died on 9 April 2016, leaving behind a legacy of theatrical works and unpublished poetry.
achievements: Established the first official national theatre group in Oman
Gold Medal for senior members of the theatre movement in the Gulf (1988)
Two Oman Gold Medals with certificates of appreciation (1990, 1993)
Arab Artist Society Lifetime Achievement Award (2016, posthumous)
Produced and directed over 15 theatrical works
Represented Oman at the Cannes International Film Festival (1981)

figures.name: Sheikh Said Bin Asalm Mohammed Al Shanfari
figures.title: Sheikh
figures.roleDescription: Father of Mohammed Al Shanfari, patriarch of the family
figures.significance: Father of the renowned dramatist Mohammed Al Shanfari and patriarch of this notable Al Shanfari family branch.
figures.biography: Sheikh Said Bin Asalm Mohammed Al Shanfari was the father of Mohammed bin Said bin Asalm Al Shanfari, the famous Omani dramatist and playwright. He was married to Saida Bint Abdulazis Al Ruwas and had at least three sons: Mohammed, Ahmed, and Abdullah. The family was based in Salalah, Dhofar, where they raised their children. As indicated by his title of Sheikh, he held a position of respect and authority within the community.

figures.title: Order of Royal Commendation Recipient
figures.roleDescription: Notable figure recognized by the Omani government
figures.significance: Recipient of the Order of Royal Commendation (Second Class) from the Sultan of Oman in 2022.
figures.biography: Saleh Mohammed al Shanfari is a contemporary member of the Al Shanfari family who has achieved recognition at the national level in Oman. In 2022, he was awarded the Order of Royal Commendation (Second Class) by the Sultan of Oman, an honor established in 2007 that recognizes distinguished service to the nation. This prestigious award places him among a select group of Omani citizens who have made significant contributions to their country.
achievements: Order of Royal Commendation (Second Class) - 2022

figures.name: Brigadier General Hafeez bin Amer Al Shanfari
figures.title: Brigadier General
figures.roleDescription: Senior military officer in the Royal Army of Oman
figures.significance: High-ranking military officer who received the Military Order of Achievement (Second Class) for distinguished military service.
figures.biography: Brigadier General Hafeez bin Amer Al Shanfari is a senior military officer in the Royal Army of Oman who has achieved distinction through his long service to the Omani military. In 2015, he was awarded the Military Order of Achievement (Second Class), an honor established in 1995 by Sultan Qaboos bin Said to recognize officers who have served for 25 years. This award, which coincided with Sultan Qaboos's 25th anniversary on the throne, recognizes his significant contribution to Oman's defense forces and his exemplary military career.
achievements: Military Order of Achievement (Second Class) - 2015

figures.name: Saeed Ahmed Lootah
figures.title: Founder and Developer
figures.roleDescription: Founder of Port Saeed locality in Dubai
figures.significance: Established Port Saeed in Dubai, naming it after the Egyptian city of Port Said.
figures.biography:
Saeed Ahmed Lootah was a prominent Emirati developer and businessman who established the Port Saeed locality in Dubai. He named this development after the Egyptian city of Port Said, which had been destroyed during the Anglo-French invasion in the Suez Crisis, demonstrating his awareness of contemporary Arab political events. Port Saeed became an important area in Dubai, strategically located along Dubai Creek and bordered by Al Khabisi to the north, Al Garhoud to the east, and Rigga Al Buteen to the west.

The locality he founded stretches between Al Garhoud Bridge and Al Maktoum Bridge and includes significant landmarks such as Deira City Centre and Dubai Creek Golf and Yacht Club. His development featured important infrastructure including a marina, golf course, and various hospitality facilities. The area has continued to evolve, with Dubai Municipality redesigning parts of the residential neighborhood after 2003, resulting in several new buildings being constructed in the area he originally established.
achievements: Founded Port Saeed locality in Dubai
Created strategic waterfront development along Dubai Creek

figures.name: Ali Lootah
figures.title: Chairman
figures.roleDescription: Chairman of Nakheel Properties
figures.significance: Served as Chairman of Nakheel, overseeing major Dubai development projects including Palm Jebel Ali.
figures.biography:
Ali Lootah served as Chairman of Nakheel, one of Dubai's major property development companies responsible for iconic projects including the Palm Islands. During his tenure, he oversaw the complex challenges facing the Palm Jebel Ali project, which had been stalled due to the 2008 financial crisis. In March 2015, he publicly confirmed that Nakheel remained committed to the Palm Jebel Ali project in the long term, though he acknowledged the difficulties facing original investors by asking "what can I do" for them.

Lootah's leadership came during a challenging period for Dubai's real estate sector, as the company dealt with the aftermath of the global financial crisis and its impact on large-scale development projects. Under his chairmanship, Nakheel had to navigate complex investor relations, particularly regarding the stalled Palm Jebel Ali development, while maintaining the company's commitment to completing its major projects. His tenure represented efforts to sustain Dubai's ambitious development vision despite significant economic headwinds.
achievements: Led Nakheel during challenging post-2008 financial crisis period
Maintained long-term commitment to major Dubai development projects

figures.name: Mohammad Abdallah Al Gaz
figures.title: Former Chairman of the UAE Central Bank
figures.roleDescription: Financier, developer, diplomat, central banker and philanthropist
figures.significance: A pioneering figure in UAE financial development and philanthropy who helped establish the country's first charitable institution.
figures.biography:
Mohamed Abdallah AlGaz was born in Al Dhagaya, Deira in 1920 to a modest family. His father, Abdulla Mohamed AlFalasi, died unexpectedly when AlGaz was an adolescent, but his mother enrolled him in the Al Ahmadiya School. During World War II famine, he temporarily settled in Bahrain with his family while working as a carpenter in Dammam, using his earnings to start a commodity trading operation.

Emerging as an enterprising trader in textiles and commodities, AlGaz entered the historic Switzerland-Dubai-India gold trade in the 1950s after partnering with Juma Al Majid to establish the famous Mohamed & Juma Al Majid company. He rose to political power as a trusted advisor of Sheikh Rashid bin Saeed al Maktoum and was nominated to the Dubai Municipal Council on July 7, 1958. After the gold trade declined, he and Al Majid secured franchise rights for international brands including General Electric, Samsung, Phillips, Opel, Pepsi, Nissan, Hyundai, and Kia before splitting their assets amicably after 11 years.

Post-federation, AlGaz served as the first Deputy Chairman of the Central Bank of the UAE from 1980 into the mid-1990s under Chairman Sheikh Suroor bin Mohammad Al Nahyan. He was instrumental in UAE's economic development and philanthropy, building schools including the Amna bint Wahb Girls School and Gamal Abdulnasser Boys School. In 1989, he developed with Juma Al Majid the UAE's first charitable society, Beit Al Khair. He died at age 96 on April 12, 2016, at Cleveland Clinic Abu Dhabi.
achievements: First Deputy Chairman of the Central Bank of the UAE (1980-1990s)
Co-founder of Beit Al Khair, UAE's first charitable society (1989)
Built the first three-story building in Dubai's history
Developed much of the property around Port Said and Hamriya in Bur Deira
UAE Pioneers Award (2018, posthumous)
President's Merit Award (2012)

figures.name: Mohammed Al Bastaki
figures.title: Architect and Construction Supervisor
figures.roleDescription: Supervised construction of Qasr Al Hosn
figures.significance: Supervised the construction of Qasr Al Hosn in 1761, the oldest stone building in Abu Dhabi.
figures.biography:
Mohammed Al Bastaki was responsible for supervising the construction of the Qasr Al Hosn (The Fortified Palace) in 1761. This historical landmark became the oldest stone building in the city of Abu Dhabi, the capital of the United Arab Emirates. The structure was originally built as a conical watchtower to defend the only freshwater well in Abu Dhabi island.

The tower that Al Bastaki supervised was later expanded into a small fort in 1793 by the then ruler, Shakhbut bin Dhiyab Al Nahyan, and became the permanent residence of the ruling Sheikh. The building took its present shape after a major extension in the late 1930s and remained the emir's palace and seat of government until 1966.
achievements: Supervised construction of Qasr Al Hosn in 1761
Created the oldest stone building in Abu Dhabi

figures.name: Shamma Al Bastaki
figures.title: Poet and Artist
figures.roleDescription: Emirati poet, artist, and cultural advocate
figures.significance: Award-winning Emirati poet and artist, Harvard graduate, and cultural ambassador who won the ADMAF Creativity Award for her poetry collection.
figures.biography:
Shamma Al Bastaki was born in Dubai in 1996 and is an Emirati poet and artist. She holds a master's degree from Harvard University and a bachelor's degree from New York University Abu Dhabi, where she double majored in Social Research and Public Policy as well as Literature and Creative Writing. She completed diplomatic training at the Anwar Gargash Diplomatic Academy, earning a PgD in Diplomacy and International Relations in 2021.

Al Bastaki has been writing poetry since she was seven years old and has written hundreds of poems. She completed her first full volume of poetry 'House to House بيت لبيت' in 2018, based on oral histories from the Dubai Creek communities. In 2019, she won the ADMAF Creativity Award for this poetry collection, an excerpt of which was published in the Asymptote Journal and taught in universities in Japan, Taiwan, and the US. She is also an ambassador for Louvre Abu Dhabi and co-founded several literary networks, including JARA Collective and Untitled Chapters. Her thesis 'Al-Majaz: A Crossing' won the Best Thesis Award at the Harvard Center for Middle Eastern Studies.
achievements: ADMAF Creativity Award winner (2019)
Harvard CMES Best Thesis Award (2022)
NYU President's Service Award (2018)
Abu Dhabi Festival Visual Arts Award – 2nd place (2016)
Co-founder of JARA Collective and literary networks

figures.name: Huda Al-Khatib
figures.title: Actress
figures.roleDescription: Emirati actress of Achomi ancestry
figures.significance: Emirati actress of Achomi ancestry who appeared in Kuwaiti television and highlighted Achomi cultural identity through her performances.
figures.biography:
Huda Al-Khatib is an Emirati actress of Achomi ancestry who has appeared in television productions across the Gulf region. She notably appeared in the Kuwaiti TV comedy drama serial 'Al-Da'la' (الدعلة) where she spoke broken Arabic mixed with Persian/Achomi, bringing attention to the cultural identity of Achomi migrants and their linguistic heritage.

In her role, she was shown bringing her giddu (گِدو; old traditional Iranian hookah) and raging out in Achomi and Persian. The show featured a full scene in the 24th Episode where her character was shown learning the Arabic language and sang the Laristani/Achomi/Bastaki song 'Ghalyon ma teshn, ghori ma chai, yar nazanin, jaye to khali' by Yousif Hadi Bastaki. Her performances highlighted the challenges faced by Achomi migrants in adapting to Arabic-speaking environments while preserving their linguistic and musical heritage.
achievements: Appeared in Kuwaiti TV series Al-Da'la
Promoted Achomi cultural identity through performance

figures.title: Medical Director
figures.roleDescription: Director of the Dubai Health Authority
figures.significance: Serves as director of the Dubai Health Authority, contributing to healthcare leadership in the UAE.
figures.biography:
Dr. Ibtesam Al-Bastaki currently serves as the director of the Dubai Health Authority, one of the key healthcare institutions in the United Arab Emirates. In her role, she oversees healthcare services and policy implementation in Dubai, contributing to the emirate's healthcare infrastructure and public health initiatives.

As part of the notable Al-Bastaki family of Achomi ancestry, she represents the community's contributions to various professional sectors in the UAE, particularly in healthcare administration and medical services.
achievements: Director of Dubai Health Authority

figures.name: Khadja Al-Bastaki
figures.title: Business Executive
figures.roleDescription: Senior Vice President of Dubai Design District
figures.significance: Serves as Senior Vice President of Dubai Design District, contributing to Dubai's creative and design sector development.
figures.biography:
Khadja Al-Bastaki serves as the Senior Vice President of Dubai Design District (d3), a strategic creative zone established to foster design, fashion, art, and luxury sectors in Dubai. In her leadership role, she oversees the development and operations of one of Dubai's key creative hubs, contributing to the emirate's diversification into creative industries.

As a member of the Al-Bastaki family, she represents the continued involvement of Achomi-descended families in Dubai's economic and cultural development, particularly in sectors that bridge traditional commerce with contemporary creative industries.
achievements: Senior Vice President of Dubai Design District

figures.name: Moein Al-Bastaki
figures.title: Television Presenter
figures.roleDescription: Emirati presenter and trick magician
figures.significance: Emirati television presenter and magician of Achomi ancestry, contributing to entertainment media in the UAE.
figures.biography:
Moein Al-Bastaki is an Emirati television presenter and trick magician of Achomi ancestry. He has worked in the entertainment industry in the United Arab Emirates, combining presentation skills with magic performance. His work contributes to the diverse entertainment landscape in the UAE.

As a member of the Al-Bastaki family, he represents the community's involvement in various sectors of Emirati society, including media and entertainment, showcasing the cultural diversity within the UAE's creative industries.
achievements: Television presenter and magician in UAE media

figures.name: Ahmed bin Abdullah bin Ibrahim Al Juffali
figures.nameAr: أحمد بن عبدالله بن إبراهيم الجفالي
figures.title: Founder and Chairman
figures.roleDescription: Founder of E. A. Juffali and Brothers, Saudi Arabia's largest privately owned enterprise
figures.significance: Founded E. A. Juffali and Brothers, the largest privately owned enterprise in Saudi Arabia, and was a main founder of the Saudi central bank (SAMA).
figures.biography:
Ahmed bin Abdullah bin Ibrahim Al Juffali was born in Mecca on 1 January 1924, into a wealthy family from Unayzah, Al-Qassim. The Al-Juffali are members of the Bani Khalid dynasty, rulers of Eastern Arabia during the early 1600s to the late 1800s. He was the third and most favored son of Sheikh Abdullah bin Ibrahim Al Juffali, and his mother was Madawi bint Ibrahim Al Obeid. Ahmed attended homeschool in his father's historic palace in the center of Mecca, where he was educated by financial scholars and merchants.

Ahmed founded E. A. Juffali and Brothers together with his brothers Ibrahim and Ali, building a gleaming office building in Jeddah that housed major international companies including Mercedes-Benz, Bosch, Dow Chemical, IBM, Siemens, and many others. He was the main founder of the Saudi Arabian Monetary Authority (SAMA), the country's central bank, and served as a member of the international advisory board of Chase Bank and on boards of Wells Fargo and IBM. Ahmed developed great interest in philanthropic causes, launching The Help Centre in 1985, a non-governmental organization dedicated to the welfare of persons with intellectual disabilities.

On 20 July 1994, Sheikh Ahmed died on the Balearic Sea in his 48-meter yacht 'TATASU' after a sudden heart attack. He married Suad bint Ibrahim Al Husseini in the early 1970s and had four children: Al-Walid, Khaled, Tarek, and Al-Maha. His fortune was inherited by his son Khaled bin Ahmed Al Juffali.
achievements: Founded E. A. Juffali and Brothers, Saudi Arabia's largest privately owned enterprise
Main founder of the Saudi Arabian Monetary Authority (SAMA)
Member of international advisory board of Chase Bank
Board member of Wells Fargo and IBM
Founded The Help Centre for persons with intellectual disabilities in 1985

figures.name: Walid Ahmed Juffali
figures.title: Chairman
figures.roleDescription: Chairman of E. A. Juffali and Brothers and multiple major Saudi companies
figures.significance: Assumed leadership of Saudi Arabia's largest privately owned enterprise after his father's death and became a prominent business figure with interests spanning multiple industries.
figures.biography:
Walid Ahmed Juffali was born in Jeddah on 30 April 1955, the son of Ahmed Abdullah Juffali, founder of E. A. Juffali and Brothers. His mother was Suad bint Ibrahim Al Husseini, granddaughter of Kamil of Jerusalem and niece of Hajj Amin of Jerusalem. After completing elementary and secondary education between Jeddah and Switzerland, he received a bachelor's degree in International Business and Political Sciences from the University of San Diego in 1977. He later earned a doctorate in neurosciences from Imperial College in 2012, with his PhD thesis entitled 'A Novel Algorithm for Detection and Prediction of Neural Anomalies.'

Walid assumed the chairmanship of E. A. Juffali and Brothers after his father's death in 1994. He also served as chairman of Saudi American Bank, The Saudi Cement Company, and sat on boards of Saudi Hollandi Bank and many other companies. In 2005, he was appointed chair of Dow Chemical Arabia and Siemens Arabia. He owned his personal investment company 'W Investments' and had significant stakes in major international companies, including 5% of Hitachi and 11% of Manchester United. Walid developed a great interest in brain theory and launched the annual brain forum conference, which by 2016 hosted over 1,400 participants from 31 countries.

Walid's personal life included high-profile divorces, including a £75 million settlement with Christina Estrada in 2016, which was the largest such settlement in English legal history at the time. He died in Zürich, Switzerland on 20 July 2016 after a lengthy battle with cancer. His estate was inherited by his brother Sheikh Khalid bin Ahmed Al Juffali, who also became the legal guardian of his five daughters.
achievements: Chairman of E. A. Juffali and Brothers, Saudi Arabia's largest privately owned enterprise
Chairman of Saudi American Bank and The Saudi Cement Company
Earned doctorate in neurosciences from Imperial College
Founded annual brain forum conference with international participation
Had significant stakes in Hitachi (5%) and Manchester United (11%)
Donated at least $3 million to the Clinton Presidential Center

figures.name: Khalid bin Ahmed Al Juffali
figures.title: Sheikh and Business Leader
figures.roleDescription: Inheritor of the Juffali business empire and legal guardian
figures.significance: Became the inheritor of Walid Juffali's estate and legal guardian of his five daughters after Walid's death in 2016.
figures.biography:
Khalid bin Ahmed Al Juffali is one of the sons of Ahmed bin Abdullah Al Juffali, founder of E. A. Juffali and Brothers. He is the full brother of Walid bin Ahmed Al Juffali and Tarek bin Ahmed Al Juffali. After his brother Walid's death in July 2016, Khalid became the inheritor of Walid's estate and took on the responsibility of being the legal guardian of Walid's five daughters.

As part of the Al-Juffali family, Khalid belongs to the wealthy and secretive family from Unayzah, Al-Qassim, who are members of the Bani Khalid dynasty. The family built one of Saudi Arabia's most successful business empires through E. A. Juffali and Brothers, which became the largest privately owned enterprise in the country.

figures.name: Tarek bin Ahmed Al Juffali
figures.title: Business Executive
figures.roleDescription: Member of the Al Juffali business family
figures.significance: One of the sons of Ahmed Al Juffali and brother to Walid and Khalid, part of the family leadership of Saudi Arabia's largest privately owned enterprise.
figures.biography:
Tarek bin Ahmed Al Juffali is one of the sons of Ahmed bin Abdullah Al Juffali, the founder of E. A. Juffali and Brothers. He is the full brother of Walid bin Ahmed Al Juffali and Khalid bin Ahmed Al Juffali. As part of the Al-Juffali family from Unayzah, Al-Qassim, Tarek belongs to the wealthy business dynasty that are members of the Bani Khalid.

The Al-Juffali family built their business empire through E. A. Juffali and Brothers, which became Saudi Arabia's largest privately owned enterprise. The family has been instrumental in introducing various international companies and industries to Saudi Arabia, including power generation, telecommunications, and vehicle manufacturing.

figures.name: Reema Juffali
figures.title: Racing Driver
figures.roleDescription: Saudi Arabian racing driver and motorsport pioneer
figures.significance: Notable Saudi Arabian racing driver who competed as a wildcard entry in the 2024 F1 Academy season at Jeddah.
figures.biography:
Reema Juffali is a Saudi Arabian racing driver born in 1992. She made history as one of the pioneering female racing drivers from Saudi Arabia, competing in GT racing before moving into single-seater competition. In 2024, she participated in the F1 Academy season as a wildcard entry for the opening round at the Jeddah Street Circuit.

During her F1 Academy debut weekend in Jeddah, Reema faced challenges typical of motorsport competition. In the first race, she was involved in a collision with Amna Al Qubaisi on lap 2, and in the second race, she crashed out on lap 6, which caused the sole safety car period of that race. Despite the difficulties, her participation represented an important milestone for women in motorsport in Saudi Arabia and the broader Middle East region.
achievements: Pioneering female racing driver from Saudi Arabia
Competed in GT racing
Wildcard entry in 2024 F1 Academy season at Jeddah

figures.name: Khalid Ali Bugshan
figures.title: Saudi Businessman
figures.roleDescription: Saudi businessman involved in art transactions and legal proceedings
figures.significance: Saudi businessman who arranged art sales and was questioned in connection with French political corruption investigations.
figures.biography:
Khalid Ali Bugshan is a Saudi businessman who became involved in high-profile French political investigations in the 2010s. He was connected to the sale of two Andries van Eertvelt paintings that were allegedly sold to a Malaysian attorney, with the transaction being arranged by Bugshan according to Claude Guéant's claims.

In March 2015, Bugshan was taken into police custody for questioning by French authorities in connection with the investigation into alleged Libyan financing of Nicolas Sarkozy's 2007 presidential campaign. While he was not charged, his arrest came on the same day as Claude Guéant, Sarkozy's former chief of staff, who claimed that money he received was payment for the art sale that Bugshan had arranged. Bugshan's involvement highlighted the complex web of international business relationships that became scrutinized during the French political corruption investigations.

figures.name: Abdullah Ahmed Bugshan
figures.title: Chairman of Hadhramaut Culture Foundation
figures.roleDescription: Saudi businessman and cultural patron funding Yemeni musical heritage projects
figures.significance: Saudi businessman who serves as Chairman of the Hadhramaut Culture Foundation and is the main sponsor of the Heritage Symphonies cultural project promoting Yemeni folk music.
figures.biography:
Abdullah Ahmed Bugshan is a prominent Saudi businessman who serves as Chairman of the Hadhramaut Culture Foundation. He is the principal financial backer of the Heritage Symphonies project, a major cultural initiative founded in 2019 that aims to promote and disseminate Yemeni folk music by integrating it into orchestral performances that combine traditional instruments with Western orchestral elements.

Bugshan's foundation has provided substantial funding for multiple international concerts, including performances in Malaysia, Egypt, France, Qatar, Kuwait, and other countries. The project has achieved significant commercial success, with concerts regularly selling out across the Arab world. However, his involvement has also drawn criticism from some Yemeni music critics who have questioned whether such generous funding might have been more effectively invested in reviving local musical institutions in Yemen, and who have suggested that his financial influence has affected the project's musical execution and vision.
achievements: Founded and chairs the Hadhramaut Culture Foundation
Principal sponsor of the internationally successful Heritage Symphonies project
Funded major orchestral concerts promoting Yemeni culture across multiple countries

figures.name: Fatemeh Khanum Alinaghi
figures.title: Princess and Mother
figures.roleDescription: Wife of Prince Abdol-Hossein Farman Farma
figures.significance: Mother of several prominent Iranian princes and princesses in the Qajar dynasty.
figures.biography:
Fatemeh Khanum Alinaghi (1900-1984) was one of the eight wives of Prince Abdol-Hossein Farman Farma, a prominent member of the Qajar dynasty and former Prime Minister of Iran. She bore him seven children between 1918 and 1927, including Princess Mahsama, Prince Jamshid, Prince Kaveh, Prince Ali Naghi, Prince Alidad, Princess Bilqis (who died young in 1927), and Prince Hafez.

As part of the extended Farman Farma family, she lived through the major political transitions of 20th century Iran, including the fall of the Qajar dynasty and the rise of the Pahlavi dynasty. Her husband was known for ensuring all his children, including daughters, received both classical Iranian training and modern Western education. Fatemeh Khanum lived for nearly eight decades, witnessing the transformation of Iran from a traditional monarchy to a modern state.

figures.name: Ali Naghi Farman Farmaian
figures.title: Prince
figures.roleDescription: Son of Prince Abdol-Hossein Farman Farma
figures.significance: Member of the prominent Qajar dynasty Farman Farma family.
figures.biography:
Prince Ali Naghi Farman Farmaian was born in 1923 as the son of Prince Abdol-Hossein Farman Farma and Fatemeh Khanum Alinaghi (Shirazi). He was part of the large Farman Farma family, one of the most influential political dynasties in Qajar and early Pahlavi Iran. His father served as the 11th Prime Minister of Iran and held numerous other high governmental positions.

Like his siblings, Ali Naghi was raised with both traditional Iranian values and modern Western education, as his father believed in preparing his children for the changing political landscape of Iran. He lived through the major transformations of 20th century Iran, including the transition from Qajar to Pahlavi rule and later the Islamic Revolution of 1979.

figures.name: Saddam Kamel Hassan al-Majid
figures.title: Head of Republican Guard
figures.roleDescription: Iraqi military officer and son-in-law of Saddam Hussein
figures.significance: He was a high-ranking military officer who served as head of the Republican Guard and later defected with his brother, providing intelligence to Western agencies.
figures.biography:
Saddam Kamel Hassan al-Majid was born on June 1, 1960, and was the first cousin once removed and son-in-law of Iraqi president Saddam Hussein. He was married to Rana Hussein, one of Saddam's daughters, and was the brother of Hussein Kamel al-Majid. He served as head of the Republican Guard until 1986, when he was removed from the position in favor of Saddam Hussein's son, Qusay Hussein. Due to his close resemblance to the Iraqi leader, he played the part of Saddam Hussein in the propagandistic film 'The Long Days'.

In 1995, he defected from Iraq with his brother Hussein Kamel and their wives, seeking refuge in Jordan. His brother provided valuable information about Iraq's weapons of mass destruction to UNSCOM, the CIA, and MI6. However, in 1996, believing Saddam's assurances that they had been pardoned, the brothers returned to Iraq. Shortly after their return on February 23, 1996, both brothers were killed in a prolonged gun battle, either by other members of their clan or soldiers loyal to Saddam, in what Iraqi authorities described as family vengeance.
achievements: Served as head of the Republican Guard
Portrayed Saddam Hussein in the film 'The Long Days'

figures.name: Hussein Kamel al-Majid
figures.title: Minister of Military Industries
figures.roleDescription: Iraqi government official responsible for weapons programs
figures.significance: He was responsible for Iraq's Chemical, Biological and Nuclear Weapons program during the 1980s and provided crucial intelligence to Western agencies after his defection.
figures.biography:
Hussein Kamel al-Majid was born in 1954 and served as Iraq's Minister of Military Industries under Saddam Hussein's regime. He was married to Raghad Hussein, Saddam's eldest daughter, in 1983, making him both a government official and a member of Saddam's inner family circle. During the 1980s, he was responsible for Iraq's Chemical, Biological and Nuclear Weapons program, giving him extensive knowledge of the country's weapons of mass destruction capabilities.

In 1995, Hussein Kamel made the significant decision to defect from Iraq along with his brother Saddam Kamel and their wives. They fled to Jordan where King Hussein granted them asylum. During his time in exile, Hussein Kamel shared valuable information about Iraq's weapons programs with UNSCOM, the CIA, and MI6. However, his plans to oust Saddam with Western support were undermined when Saddam decided to reveal weapons information himself. In 1996, believing promises of pardons and facing increasing isolation in Jordan, the brothers returned to Iraq where they were killed in February 1996, allegedly by fellow clan members who declared them traitors.
achievements: Minister of Military Industries
Responsible for Iraq's WMD programs in the 1980s
Provided intelligence on Iraqi weapons to Western agencies

figures.name: Kamel Hassan al-Majid
figures.title: Ba'ath Party Official
figures.roleDescription: Iraqi Ba'ath Party official and father of Hussein and Saddam Kamel
figures.significance: He was a Ba'ath Party official who became marginalized in later years and was killed alongside his sons in the 1996 firefight.
figures.biography:
Kamel Hassan al-Majid was born around 1920 and served as a Ba'ath Party official during Saddam Hussein's regime. He was the father of Hussein Kamel al-Majid and Saddam Kamel Hassan al-Majid, both of whom became high-ranking officials in Saddam's government and married into Saddam's family. Kamel was the brother of Hussein Abd al-Majid, making him part of the extended al-Majid family network that held significant influence in Ba'athist Iraq.

In his later years, Kamel became marginalized within the Ba'ath Party structure, losing much of his earlier influence. When his sons Hussein and Saddam Kamel defected to Jordan in 1995 and then returned to Iraq in 1996, Kamel was caught up in the violent aftermath. On February 23, 1996, he was killed alongside his sons and other family members in a firefight that Iraqi authorities described as clan vengeance for the brothers' betrayal of the regime.
achievements: Ba'ath Party official

figures.name: Stanley Kamel
figures.title: American Actor
figures.roleDescription: Television and film actor best known for role as Dr. Charles Kroger
figures.significance: He was an American actor best known for playing Dr. Charles Kroger, the psychiatrist to the main character in the popular USA Network television series Monk.
figures.biography:
Stanley Kamel was born on January 1, 1943, to a Jewish family and was raised in South River, New Jersey. He attended Rutgers Preparatory School and Boston University, graduating with a BFA in 1965. Kamel started his acting career off-Broadway before transitioning to television in 1968. Over the decades, he appeared in numerous episodic television shows including The Mod Squad, Barney Miller, Kojak, Lou Grant, Hill Street Blues, L.A. Law, Murder, She Wrote, Star Trek: The Next Generation, and many others.

Kamel had recurring roles on several major television series, including Melrose Place in 1994 as Bruce Teller, the CEO of D&D Advertising, and Beverly Hills, 90210 as organized crime figure Anthony Marchette. However, he was best known for his role as Dr. Charles Kroger in the USA Network television series Monk, playing the patient and supportive psychiatrist to Adrian Monk (Tony Shalhoub) from 2002 until his death in 2008. Kamel never married and had no children. On April 8, 2008, he was found dead of a heart attack in his Hollywood Hills home at age 65. The seventh season premiere of Monk was dedicated to his memory.
achievements: Starred as Dr. Charles Kroger in the TV series Monk
Appeared in numerous popular television series over several decades

figures.name: Hussein Kamel
figures.title: Sultan of Egypt
figures.roleDescription: Ruler of Egypt during the British protectorate
figures.significance: He was the first person to hold the title of Sultan of Egypt since 1517 and ruled during the critical period when Egypt became a British protectorate during World War I.
figures.biography:
Hussein Kamel was born on November 21, 1853, as the second son of Khedive Ismail Pasha, who ruled Egypt from 1863 to 1879. He was declared Sultan of Egypt on December 19, 1914, after the occupying British forces had deposed his nephew, Khedive Abbas Hilmi II, on November 5, 1914. This marked the end of de jure Ottoman sovereignty over Egypt, which had been largely nominal since Muhammad Ali's seizure of power in 1805. Though presented as the re-establishment of the pre-Ottoman Egyptian sultanate, the newly created Sultanate of Egypt was effectively a British protectorate.

During his reign, Hussein Kamel faced significant challenges, including two assassination attempts in 1915. The first occurred on April 9, 1915, when an assassin targeted his carriage in Cairo, and the second on July 9, 1915, when a bomb was thrown at his carriage as he traveled to Friday prayers. Hussein Kamel died on October 9, 1917. Upon his death, his only son, Prince Kamal el Dine Hussein, declined the succession, and Hussein Kamel's brother Ahmed Fuad ascended the throne as Fuad I.
achievements: First Sultan of Egypt since 1517
Founder of the Order of Muhammad Ali
Founder of the Order of Ismail
Founder of the Order of the Nile

figures.name: Kamel El Basha
figures.title: Palestinian Actor
figures.roleDescription: Actor, theatre director, teacher, and translator
figures.significance: He is a prominent Palestinian actor who won the prestigious Volpi Cup for Best Actor at the Venice International Film Festival for his performance in 'The Insult'.
figures.biography:
Kamel El Basha was born around 1962 in Malha (al-Maliha), then a thriving Palestinian village, now a suburb in south-western Jerusalem, Israel. He recalls being five years old and fleeing his home with his family during the 1967 war. As a young man, he was imprisoned by the Israeli authorities for two years. He studied theatre from 1979 to 1983 and later studied theatre in Baghdad, Iraq. After his release from prison, he began his career as a stage actor, making a notable debut in the Arabic version of Brecht's 'The Exception and the Rule'.

El Basha served as artistic director of the Palestinian National Theater in Jerusalem from 2007 to 2011, and as artistic director of Quds Art from 2012. He came to international attention for his role as Palestinian refugee Yasser Abdallah Salameh in the 2017 film 'The Insult' by Lebanese director Ziad Doueiri, for which he won the Volpi Cup for Best Actor at the 74th Venice International Film Festival. He has appeared in several other notable films including 'Love, Theft and Other Entanglements' (2015), 'The Reports on Sarah and Saleem' (2018), and the 2023 British heist series 'Culprits'. As of 2024, he lives in East Jerusalem with his Palestinian Christian wife.
achievements: Won Volpi Cup for Best Actor at Venice International Film Festival 2017
Artistic director of Palestinian National Theater (2007-2011)
Best Actor prize at Series Mania for 'House of Gods' (2024)

figures.name: Nourah Abdul Aziz Al-Khereiji
figures.title: Journalist
figures.roleDescription: Arab News journalist and interviewer
figures.significance: Notable journalist who conducted interviews with prominent cultural figures for Arab News.
figures.biography:
Nourah Abdul Aziz Al-Khereiji worked as a journalist for Arab News, a prominent English-language daily newspaper in Saudi Arabia. In 2001, she conducted a significant interview with former Egyptian actress Shams al-Baroudi during the Al-Madinah Festival. This interview was particularly notable as it captured al-Baroudi's reflections on her acting career and her religious transformation, with the former actress describing her acting era as 'the time of ignorance,' referencing the pre-Islamic period.

Al-Khereiji's journalistic work contributed to documenting important cultural and social conversations in the Arab world, particularly regarding the intersection of entertainment, religion, and social conservatism in Middle Eastern society.
achievements: Conducted notable interview with Egyptian actress Shams al-Baroudi at the 2001 Al-Madinah Festival

figures.name: Surachai Danwattananusorn (Surachai Saedan)
figures.title: Political Activist and Former Political Prisoner
figures.roleDescription: Thai political activist, communist party member, and anti-monarchist leader
figures.significance: Last member of the Communist Party of Thailand to be pardoned and founder of the Red Siam political movement.
figures.biography:
Born Surachai Saedan on December 24, 1942, in Thaphaya Tumbol, Pak Phanang District, Nakhon Si Thammarat Province, he was the son of Yokyuan and Somchao Saedan. He became well known after the Thammasat University massacre as a prominent member of the Communist Party of Thailand. He was sentenced to death for murder and participation in a train robbery but received a royal pardon in 1988, making him the last CPT member to be pardoned.

After his release, Surachai entered mainstream politics, serving as a committee member of the Thai Rak Thai Party and later founding the Red Siam political movement with Jakrapob Penkair. He was known for his anti-monarchist views, which resulted in multiple arrests under Thailand's lèse majesté laws. He was sentenced to 7 years and 6 months in prison in 2012 but received a royal pardon in October 2013.

Following the Thai military coup of 2014, Surachai went into exile in neighboring Laos. On December 10, 2018, he disappeared from Vientiane along with two aides, who were later found murdered. He is believed to have been killed as well, representing part of a broader pattern of forced disappearances of Thai activists in exile.
achievements: Last member of Communist Party of Thailand to receive royal pardon
Founded Red Siam political movement
Served as Thai Rak Thai Party committee member

figures.name: Ahlam Aref Ahmad al-Tamimi
figures.title: Convicted Terrorist
figures.roleDescription: Jordanian national convicted for assisting in the Sbarro restaurant suicide bombing
figures.significance: Known for assisting in carrying out the Sbarro restaurant suicide bombing in Jerusalem in 2001, which caused 145 casualties including 16 fatalities.
figures.biography:
Ahlam Aref Ahmad al-Tamimi was born in 1980 in Zarqa, Jordan, and originates from Nabi Salih, a Palestinian village in the central West Bank. She was a journalism student at Birzeit University in the West Bank when she became involved in terrorist activities. At age 20, she helped plan and participated in the Sbarro restaurant suicide bombing on August 9, 2001, escorting suicide bomber Izz al-Din Shuheil al-Masri to the target location. She used disguise techniques and her journalism skills to deflect attention, wearing a dress to appear like a 'Jewish tourist' rather than an Arab.

After the bombing, which caused 145 casualties including 16 fatalities (half of them children), Tamimi reported on the attack in her part-time journalism job. She was convicted by an Israeli military tribunal and received 16 consecutive life sentences plus 15 years in prison. However, she was released in October 2011 as part of the Gilad Shalit prisoner exchange and exiled to Jordan. Since her release, she has hosted a Jordanian talk show called 'Nasim Al-Ahrar' (Breeze of the Free) on Hamas-affiliated Al-Quds TV, dealing with Palestinian prisoners in Israeli prisons. In 2013, the U.S. Justice Department filed criminal charges against her, and there is a $5 million reward for her capture.

figures.name: Ali Al-Tamimi
figures.title: Computational Biologist and Islamic Teacher
figures.roleDescription: American computational biologist and Islamic teacher convicted of soliciting treason
figures.significance: Convicted of soliciting treason and attempting to contribute services to the Taliban, sentenced to life in prison in 2005, though his conviction was later overturned on First Amendment grounds.
figures.biography:
Ali Al-Tamimi was born on December 14, 1963, and grew up in the Palisades neighborhood of Washington, DC, where he attended Georgetown Day School until age fifteen. His father was an attorney and cultural attaché at the embassy of the Kingdom of Iraq, while his mother was a mental health specialist with a doctorate in special education. At age fifteen in 1978, his parents moved the family to Riyadh, Saudi Arabia for three years to expose them to their Arabic and Islamic heritage, where he attended Manarat Riyadh High School and became influenced by Salafism under the teaching of Bilal Phillips.

Returning to the United States in 1981, Al-Timimi enrolled in a premed program at The George Washington University while networking with local Muslim community groups. He later returned to Saudi Arabia in 1987 to study for a year at the Islamic University of Madinah under Abdul-Aziz bin Baz. Back in the US, he earned a second undergraduate degree in computer science from the University of Maryland and later a Ph.D. in computational biology from George Mason University in 2004. He cofounded an Islamic center in Falls Church, Virginia called Dar al-Arqam and became known as 'arguably the first American born activist Salafi preacher.' In 2005, he was convicted of soliciting treason based on comments he allegedly made to followers after 9/11, receiving a mandatory lifetime prison sentence. After twenty years of legal proceedings, his conviction was overturned by the 4th Circuit Court of Appeals in January 2026 on First Amendment grounds.
achievements: Ph.D. in computational biology
Scientific publications on cancer research
Founded Dar al-Arqam Islamic center

figures.name: Ahed Tamimi
figures.title: Palestinian Activist
figures.roleDescription: Palestinian activist from the village of Nabi Salih
figures.significance: Known for confronting Israeli soldiers and became a symbol of Palestinian resistance, gaining international attention after a viral slapping incident in 2017.
figures.biography:
Ahed Tamimi was born on January 31, 2001, in Nabi Salih, a small village in the West Bank to activist Bassem Tamimi and his wife Nariman. Her father is a cousin of Ahlam Tamimi, known for assisting in the Sbarro restaurant bombing. From an early age, she has been involved in protests against Israeli settlements and detention of Palestinians. At age 11, she was commended by Palestinian president Mahmoud Abbas for attempting to intervene during her mother's arrest in August 2012, and gained international media attention when confronting an Israeli soldier who arrested her older brother.

On December 15, 2017, during a demonstration in Nabi Salih, Tamimi, along with her mother and cousin, was filmed slapping, kicking, and shoving Israeli soldiers outside their home. This occurred after her 15-year-old cousin Mohammed Tamimi was shot in the head with a rubber-coated steel bullet during the protest. The footage went viral, leading to her arrest on December 19. She agreed to a plea bargain and served eight months in prison, being released on July 29, 2018. In November 2023, she was arrested again on suspicion of inciting violence and held for almost three weeks before being released on November 29 as part of a hostage exchange agreement between Israel and Hamas. She has been described as one of the new symbols of Palestinian resistance and published a memoir titled 'They Called Me a Lioness' in 2022.
achievements: Published memoir 'They Called Me a Lioness'
Symbol of Palestinian resistance
International recognition as activist

figures.name: Al-Qa'qa' ibn Amr al-Tamimi
figures.nameAr: القعقاع بن عمرو بن مالك التميمي الراعي
figures.title: Arab Muslim Commander and General
figures.roleDescription: Commander and general in the Rashidun army from the tribe of Banu Tamim
figures.significance: Known as a successful military commander who took part in the victorious battles of Yarmouk against the Byzantine Empire and al-Qadisiyyah against the Sasanian Empire.
figures.biography:
Al-Qa'qa' ibn Amr al-Tamimi was an Arab Muslim commander and general in the Rashidun army who belonged to the tribe of Banu Tamim. He and his tribe converted to Islam possibly during the time of Ahnaf ibn Qais, though for a brief period he and other Tamim joined the force of the false prophetess Sajah bint al-Harith before she was subdued during the Ridda wars. He later carried on a successful military campaign under Khalid bin Walid, helping to suppress another false prophet, Tulayha, in the Battle of Buzakha.

Al-Qa'qa' took part in numerous important battles during the Muslim conquest of Iraq and Syria, including the Battle of Chains where he aided Khalid ibn al-Walid in defeating the Sassanid champion Hormuzan. He served under Khalid in the famous battle of Yarmouk against the Byzantine Empire as part of the mobile guard elite cavalry. The Caliph Abu Bakr praised him as equal to a thousand men, so Caliph Umar sent only Qa'qa' and a handful of bodyguards in the first wave of reinforcements to Al-Qadisiyyah. At the Battle of al-Qadisiyyah in November 636, he played a crucial role in the Muslim victory against the Sasanian Empire, using ingenious tactics such as disguising camels to look like monsters to frighten the Sassanid horses and leading successful charges that ultimately routed the Persian army.
achievements: Participated in Battle of Yarmouk
Led forces at Battle of al-Qadisiyyah
Praised by Caliph Abu Bakr as equal to a thousand men

figures.name: Saleh al-Fawzan
figures.title: Grand Mufti of Saudi Arabia
figures.roleDescription: Islamic scholar and fourth Grand Mufti of Saudi Arabia
figures.significance: Senior Salafi scholar who became Grand Mufti of Saudi Arabia in 2025.
figures.biography: Saleh al-Fawzan was born on 28 September 1935 in Ash-Shamasiyyah in the Al-Qassim Province of Saudi Arabia. He belongs to the Al-Wadain branch of the Al-Shammās clan of the Ad-Dawāsir tribe. His father died during his early childhood, and he was raised by his family. He learned the Quran from local mosque imam Ḥamūd ibn Sulaymān At-Tilāl. Al-Fawzan completed his education at Al-Faysaliyyah School in Buraydah in 1952 and was appointed as a primary school teacher. He then graduated from the Ma'had Al-'Ilmī in Buraydah in 1958 and from the College of Sharīʿah in Riyadh in 1962. He obtained a master's degree and doctorate in fiqh, focusing on inheritance law and Islamic food rulings respectively. After completing his doctorate, he taught at various Islamic institutions and served as head of Saudi Arabia's Supreme Court of Justice. In 2013, he was appointed as a member of the Council of Senior Scholars. On 22 October 2025, King Salman appointed al-Fawzan as Grand Mufti of Saudi Arabia, succeeding Abdulaziz Al al-Sheikh.
achievements: Appointed Grand Mufti of Saudi Arabia in 2025
Member of Council of Senior Scholars
Head of Saudi Arabia's Supreme Court of Justice
Director of Higher Institute of Judiciary

figures.name: Ali Abdullah Saleh
figures.title: President of Yemen
figures.roleDescription: First president of unified Yemen and former president of North Yemen
figures.significance: Long-serving Yemeni president who ruled for over three decades before being killed by Houthis in 2017.
figures.biography: Ali Abdullah Saleh was born on 21 March 1947 to a poor Zaydi family in Beit al-Ahmar village from the Sanhan clan, southeast of Sanaa. He joined the Royal Armed Forces in 1958 and participated in the 1962 military coup that established the Yemen Arab Republic. After President Ahmad al-Ghashmi was assassinated in 1978, Saleh was elected president of North Yemen on 17 July 1978. He survived multiple coup attempts and consolidated power through a patronage system involving his family and tribal allies. When North and South Yemen unified in 1990, Saleh became president of the new Republic of Yemen, defeating southern separatists in a 1994 civil war. He was re-elected in 1999 and 2006, ruling through increasingly autocratic means. During the 2011 Arab Spring, massive protests forced his resignation in 2012, and he was succeeded by Abdrabbuh Mansur Hadi. Saleh later allied with the Houthis during the Yemeni civil war but broke with them in December 2017. On 4 December 2017, he was killed by Houthi fighters while attempting to flee Sanaa.
achievements: President of North Yemen (1978-1990)
First President of unified Yemen (1990-2012)
Survived multiple coup attempts
Achieved Yemeni unification in 1990
figures.birthPlace: Beit al-Ahmar village, Sanhan District, Yemen

figures.name: Ahmed Ali Abdullah Saleh al-Ahmar
figures.nameAr: أحمد علي عبد الله صالح الأحمر
figures.title: Former Republican Guard Commander
figures.roleDescription: Eldest son of Ali Abdullah Saleh and former military commander
figures.significance: Former commander of Yemen's Republican Guard and eldest son of long-serving president Ali Abdullah Saleh.
figures.biography: Ahmed Ali Abdullah Saleh al-Ahmar was born on July 25, 1972, the eldest son of former Yemeni President Ali Abdullah Saleh. His mother died when he was young, and before his father's resignation, he was widely seen as being groomed to eventually replace him as president. Ahmed commanded approximately 80,000 troops of the Republican Guard unit of the Yemeni Army and was involved in corruption through his ties to the Shibam Holding Company in 2008. After President Hadi came to power in 2012, Ahmed refused to relinquish control of long-range missiles to the Defense Ministry in December 2012. In response, President Hadi restructured the military in December 2012, effectively dissolving the Republican Guard and rendering Ahmed's position unnecessary. He was appointed as Yemen's Ambassador to the United Arab Emirates in May 2013, but was dismissed in March 2015 following the outbreak of the Yemeni Civil War. The UAE terminated his ambassadorship in April 2015 and he was reportedly placed under house arrest in Abu Dhabi. After his father was killed by the Houthis in 2017, Ahmed declared his animosity against them.
achievements: Commander of Republican Guard (80,000 troops)
Ambassador to United Arab Emirates (2013-2015)

figures.name: Amrullah Saleh
figures.title: First Vice President of Afghanistan
figures.roleDescription: Former head of Afghan intelligence and vice president
figures.significance: Former head of Afghanistan's National Directorate of Security who became first vice president and proclaimed himself caretaker president after the Taliban takeover in 2021.
figures.biography: Amrullah Saleh was born on 15 October 1972 in the Panjshir region of Afghanistan to an ethnic Tajik family. Orphaned at age 7, he joined the mujahideen in 1990 to avoid conscription and fought under Ahmad Shah Massoud. He became head of the Northern Alliance's international liaison office in Dushanbe, Tajikistan in 1997. After the Taliban's fall, he was appointed head of the National Directorate of Security (NDS) by President Hamid Karzai in December 2004. As intelligence chief, he initiated structural reforms, sent agents to infiltrate Taliban operations in Pakistan, and provided intelligence about bin Laden's possible location in Abbottabad in 2006. He resigned in June 2010 due to disagreements with Karzai over Taliban policy and founded the Basej-e Milli political movement. President Ashraf Ghani appointed him as acting Interior Minister in December 2018, and he became First Vice President in February 2020. After the Taliban takeover of Kabul on 15 August 2021, Saleh proclaimed himself caretaker president and supported the National Resistance Front in Panjshir Valley before fleeing to Tajikistan in September 2021.
achievements: Head of National Directorate of Security (2004-2010)
First Vice President of Afghanistan (2020-2021)
Founded Basej-e Milli political movement
Survived multiple assassination attempts

figures.name: Aguila Saleh Issa Gueider
figures.title: Speaker of Libyan House of Representatives
figures.roleDescription: Libyan jurist and politician serving as parliamentary speaker
figures.significance: Speaker of Libya's House of Representatives who served as head of state during the second Libyan civil war from 2014-2021.
figures.biography: Aguila Saleh Issa Gueider was born on 11 January 1944 in the town of Al Qubbah, Libya. Following the 2014 Libyan parliamentary election, he was elected as Parliament Speaker but was forced to flee to Tobruk along with the rest of the Libyan House of Representatives after Tripoli was seized by militias. As Speaker, he effectively served as head of state of Libya from 2014 to 2021 under the Tobruk-based government, which was recognized by the international community as the legitimate government during the second Libyan civil war until 2016. On 20 February 2015, his residence was targeted in bombing by ISIL militants in Al Qubbah, resulting in at least 40 deaths in what became known as the Al Qubbah bombings. ISIL claimed the attacks were retaliation for Egyptian military intervention in Libya. On 20 September 2021, Aguila Saleh announced his intention to temporarily withdraw from parliament to run as a candidate for president in the 2021 Libyan general election.
achievements: Speaker of Libyan House of Representatives since 2014
Served as head of state during Libyan civil war (2014-2021)
Representative of Al Qubbah town

figures.name: Abdul Elah al-Shaya
figures.title: Yemeni Journalist
figures.roleDescription: Journalist who interviewed Al-Qaeda figures
figures.significance: Yemeni journalist who conducted significant interviews with high-profile terrorist figures including Anwar al-Awlaki.
figures.biography:
Abdul Elah al-Shaya is a Yemeni journalist who gained international attention for his interviews with prominent terrorist figures. In December 2009, he reported on a U.S.-Yemeni airstrike in eastern Shabwa that was intended to target al-Qaeda members. Al-Shaya received a phone call from Anwar al-Awlaki on December 28, 2009, in which al-Awlaki reported that he was well and had not attended the al-Qaeda meeting that was targeted in the strike.

Al-Shaya played a significant role in providing information about al-Awlaki's activities and whereabouts during a critical period when the cleric was being hunted by Yemeni and U.S. authorities. He stated publicly that al-Awlaki was not tied to al-Qaeda, providing a contrasting perspective to official government positions. His journalistic work provided rare insights into the activities and statements of one of the most wanted terrorist figures of the time.
achievements: Conducted interviews with high-profile terrorist figures
Provided international media coverage of U.S.-Yemeni counterterrorism operations

figures.name: Uzi Shaya
figures.title: Former Israeli Intelligence Officer
figures.roleDescription: Former Mossad agent and counterterrorism official
figures.significance: Former Israeli Mossad agent who conducted counterterrorism operations and was involved in efforts to combat terrorist financing through Chinese banks.
figures.biography:
Uzi Shaya is a former Israeli intelligence officer who served as a counterterrorism official with the Mossad. He conducted extensive counterterrorism operations, including traveling to China dozens of times in his official capacity to work on disrupting terrorist financing networks. Shaya was particularly involved in Israeli efforts to combat terrorist financing through Bank of China accounts that were allegedly being used to fund terrorist activities.

Following the 2006 Tel Aviv shawarma restaurant bombing that killed American teenager Daniel Wultz, the Israeli government initially supported the Wultz family's legal case against Bank of China and was prepared to allow Shaya to testify about his knowledge of Israeli-Chinese meetings regarding terrorist financing. However, after Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu's 2013 state visit to Beijing, Israeli policy changed, and the government prevented Shaya from testifying in U.S. court proceedings, claiming his testimony would jeopardize Israeli national security interests. U.S. District Judge Shira Scheindlin ultimately quashed a subpoena against Shaya, ruling that Israel had not waived his immunity regarding information he learned in his official capacity.
achievements: Conducted extensive counterterrorism operations for Israeli intelligence
Led efforts to combat terrorist financing networks in China

figures.name: Maha Al-Ghunaim
figures.title: Businesswoman and Co-founder of Global Investment House
figures.roleDescription: Chair and Managing Director of GIH (Global Investment House)
figures.significance: Co-founded and led one of Kuwait's major investment firms, managing billions in assets across international markets.
figures.biography: Maha Al-Ghunaim is a prominent Kuwaiti businesswoman who graduated from San Francisco State University in 1982. Earlier in her career, she served as assistant general manager of asset management at the Kuwait Investment Company. In 1998, she co-founded GIH (Global Investment House), which became a significant player in the regional investment landscape. Under her leadership as chair and managing director, GIH was listed on the London Stock Exchange in 2008. By 2006, GIH managed approximately $7 billion in assets, offering a variety of different funds and venturing into investments with India, China and Turkey. In 2005, GIH was responsible for aiding Lebanese telecom Investcom's establishment in London and Dubai with $200 million in funds. According to Bloomberg, she served as global chairperson of GIH from March 2007 to September 2014, until the closure of the firm on April 17, 2017. In 2010, she was named 94th in Bloomberg's Power Women 100 list. She also heads the Kuwaiti chapter of Young Arab Leaders, a pan-Arab organization which aims to develop education and entrepreneurship in Kuwait.
achievements: Co-founded Global Investment House (GIH) in 1998
Led GIH's listing on London Stock Exchange in 2008
Managed $7 billion in assets through GIH by 2006
Named 94th in Bloomberg's Power Women 100 list in 2010
Heads Kuwaiti chapter of Young Arab Leaders organization

figures.name: Dr. Ghadah G. Al-Ghunaim
figures.title: Board Member of King Abdulaziz Center for National Dialogue
figures.roleDescription: Member of the Board of Trustees at KACND
figures.significance: Serves as a board member of Saudi Arabia's national dialogue institution, contributing to intellectual discourse and national unity initiatives.
figures.biography: Dr. Ghadah G. Al-Ghunaim serves as a member of the Board of Trustees at the King Abdulaziz Center for National Dialogue (KACND), an important Saudi institution established in 2003. The Center was created to promote intellectual dialogue between Saudi citizens and different segments of Saudi society, with the goal of strengthening national unity and combating extremism.
